proposals.title: Shielded Online Learning: A Bandit Framework for Adversarially Robust Sensor Stream Integration
proposals.motivation: Deploying online learning systems in physical environments, such as sensor networks for safety-critical monitoring, exposes them to data poisoning and deceptive concept drift from intelligent adversaries. Pre-2026, methods like conformal prediction and human feedback (e.g., AlpacaFarm) address reliability, but robust online integration of streaming sensor data remains an open challenge, especially when adversaries can manipulate sensor placements or data distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: An online multi-armed bandit architecture, where one arm acts as a protective shield using conformal prediction and temporal consistency checks, can enable robust continuous learning by safely filtering adversarial anomalies and distributional shifts in real-time sensor streams.
proposals.method: We will design an ensemble bandit system where the primary arm is a detection model updated via online gradient descent. A specialized shielding arm will employ inductive conformal prediction with non-conformity scores based on model atypicality and temporal auto-correlation of sensor features. Data points are only passed to the primary learner if they fall within a dynamically adjusted prediction set and pass a consistency check with a short-term historical buffer; otherwise, they are quarantined for potential human-in-the-loop analysis, inspired by feedback frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for adversarially robust online learning from sensor streams, bridging optimal sensor placement with runtime security. It will deliver a concrete algorithm with theoretical safety bounds and empirical validation on simulated and real-world sensor datasets under attack.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a shielding bandit arm for real-time adversarial filtering within an online learning loop is novel, moving beyond static outlier detection or post-hoc analysis to proactive, in-stream protection for continuous model adaptation.

proposals.title: Adaptive Robust Learning: Guaranteeing Performance Against an Evolving Threat Model
proposals.motivation: Robust optimization provides performance guarantees against a pre-defined uncertainty set, but real-world adversaries and distribution shifts are dynamic and unknown. Static sets lead to brittle guarantees, as seen in LLMs vulnerable to novel adversarial prompts or atypical inputs. This gap limits the reliable deployment of AI systems in high-stakes, open-world scenarios where threats evolve.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding robust optimization within an adaptive outer loop that uses adversarial exploration and online monitoring, we can create a system whose formal performance guarantees expand to cover a growing, empirically discovered set of failure modes, overcoming the limitations of static uncertainty sets.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tiered algorithm. The inner loop performs robust optimization (e.g., distributionally robust optimization or adversarial training) against a current uncertainty set parameterized as a convex set or a generative model. The outer loop uses an adversarial explorer—inspired by AlpacaFarm's simulation and methods for exploiting LLM behavior—to probe the current robust model for performance gaps. Discovered failures (atypical inputs, adversarial examples) are rigorously incorporated into the uncertainty set via online convex optimization or by updating the parameters of a variational family, ensuring the inner guarantee remains valid. The process is guided by a performance monitor quantifying regret relative to the expanding threat model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for building AI systems that are both *provably* and *practically* robust, with guarantees that adapt over time. It contributes a new methodology for closing the gap between theoretical robustness certificates and the open-world challenge of novel adversaries, directly advancing reliable model deployment.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration of formal robust optimization with adaptive, exploratory learning from human feedback and adversarial interaction, moving beyond static sets or one-time adversarial training to create an *evolving* certificate of performance.

proposals.title: Anchors from Within: Reconstructing Local Decision Boundaries via Internal State Analysis
proposals.motivation: Anchor explanations provide intuitive, rule-based explanations for model predictions but require extensive, often impractical, query access to the model for input perturbation. This limits their use for large, expensive models like LLMs or in settings where query access is restricted (e.g., third-party APIs, proprietary systems). A method to compute anchors without explicit perturbation would greatly enhance the applicability and scalability of this interpretability technique.
proposals.hypothesis: The internal state (activations, gradients, attention patterns) of a single forward pass for an input contains sufficient information to locally reconstruct the model's decision function, enabling the accurate simulation of output changes for input perturbations and thus the identification of anchor features without explicit querying.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that, given an input and a model, extracts a rich representation of its internal state. Using this state, we will train a lightweight, local surrogate model (e.g., a small MLP or linear model conditioned on the state) to predict output changes for hypothesized input perturbations. This surrogate will be trained via meta-learning on a held-out set of model states and known perturbations, learning to map from internal state to a local decision boundary. The trained surrogate will then be used to computationally identify the minimal set of features that act as an anchor for the original prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of 'query-free' anchor explanations, significantly broadening their applicability. It will contribute a novel framework for model interpretability that treats internal states as a rich source for inferring decision logic, bridging the gap between post-hoc explanation and mechanistic analysis.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct analogy to passive observation in physical sciences and its departure from the query-based paradigm of all prior anchor explanation methods. It uniquely leverages the full internal state of a single forward pass to simulate the perturbation process computationally.

proposals.title: Influence Maximization for Black-Box Anchor Discovery: Finding Sufficient Conditions for Model Decisions via Static Data Analysis
proposals.motivation: As complex models like LLMs are deployed in high-stakes settings, providing faithful explanations for their decisions is critical for trust and debugging. Existing anchor explanation methods rely heavily on querying the model with perturbed inputs, which is computationally expensive, can be infeasible for non-differentiable or access-restricted models, and risks generating out-of-distribution samples. A method to derive sufficient-condition explanations using only the model's static predictions on the original data would be more efficient and broadly applicable.
proposals.hypothesis: The decision logic for a specific model prediction can be framed as an influence diffusion process over latent feature associations, such that the minimal set of features maximizing the prediction's probability—identified via influence maximization on the static prediction graph—constitutes a valid, high-precision anchor explanation.
proposals.method: We will treat the model's predictions on the training or reference dataset as a bipartite graph between input features (or their embeddings) and output decisions. Using pre-2026 influence maximization algorithms (e.g., IMM or TIM), we will identify the minimal set of input features that, when fixed, maximize the 'influence'—modeled as the conditional probability—of the target prediction. This involves constructing a probabilistic graphical model from co-occurrence statistics of features and predictions, then applying constrained seed set optimization to find the anchor set without generating new perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, query-efficient framework for generating sufficient-condition explanations for any black-box model, requiring only its static predictions. It will extend the theory of anchor explanations by linking them to influence maximization and graph-based analysis, offering a new tool for model interpretability that is especially valuable for restricted or non-differentiable systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the anchor discovery problem by using analytical graph-based influence maximization on existing predictions, bypassing the need for perturbation-based querying entirely—a significant departure from prior work like the original Anchors method or LIME.

proposals.title: Causal Necessity Explanations: Unifying Probabilistic Necessity and Causal Validity for Robust LLM Interpretability
proposals.motivation: Current explanation methods for large language models (LLMs), like feature attribution, often identify features that are statistically associated but not causally necessary for a prediction. These explanations can be brittle under distribution shifts and may reflect spurious correlations, undermining trust and safety, especially in high-stakes applications. A method that rigorously grounds probabilistic necessity in causal validity is needed to produce robust, actionable explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating causal discovery and intervention analysis with probabilistic necessity measures, we can generate model explanations that are both necessary within the data distribution and demonstrably causal, leading to significantly improved robustness against adversarial and out-of-distribution inputs compared to purely correlational methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first uses probabilistic necessity scores (e.g., based on Sufficient Explanations or conditional probability) to identify candidate feature sets. We will then apply causal discovery techniques (e.g., PC algorithm, Fast Causal Inference) on model activations and inputs to infer a causal graph. Finally, we will perform targeted interventions (e.g., using do-calculus or counterfactual generation via methods like DiCE) to test the causal necessity of the identified features, pruning those where interventions do not alter the model's prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel class of causally-grounded explanation tools that are more reliable and robust than current state-of-the-art methods. It will advance the field of interpretable AI by providing a formal, testable bridge between statistical association and causal mechanism for complex models like LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored probabilistic sufficiency/necessity or causal explanation separately, this proposal uniquely fuses them into a single, rigorous framework that explicitly tests for and eliminates spurious probabilistic necessities through causal intervention, a non-obvious synthesis given existing literature.

proposals.title: From Sufficient to Necessary: Probabilistic Necessary Explanations for Reliable Model Interpretation
proposals.motivation: Current post-hoc explanation methods, like LIME and SHAP, identify features that are locally sufficient for a model's prediction. However, for high-stakes decisions and robust human-AI collaboration, users need to know which features are necessary—if absent, the prediction would likely change. This gap limits the reliability and actionable insight of explanations, especially for complex models like LLMs where spurious correlations can create sufficient but non-causal rationales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by estimating the conditional probability of a prediction change given the removal of a feature set, we can generate probabilistically necessary explanations that are more robust and faithful to model behavior than purely sufficient ones.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework building on concepts from algorithmic recourse and counterfactual explanations. For a given input and model prediction, we will use a masked language model or a variational autoencoder to sample from the distribution of plausible alternatives where a candidate explanatory feature set is absent or altered. We will then estimate the probability of prediction change across these samples, defining probabilistic necessity. The approach will be integrated into an interactive system, inspired by AlpacaFarm, to collect human feedback on explanation utility and iteratively refine the necessity criterion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal definition and practical method for generating probabilistically necessary explanations, moving beyond the sufficiency paradigm. It will contribute a new evaluation benchmark for explanation faithfulness based on necessity and offer insights into making LLM decision processes more reliable and interpretable for human users.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on 'necessary and sufficient' explanations exists in logic-based frameworks, applying a probabilistic necessity criterion to complex, high-dimensional models like LLMs is novel. This bridges the gap between rigorous counterfactual reasoning and scalable, practical explanation tools for modern AI systems.

proposals.title: Anchored Explanations: High-Coverage, Probabilistically Guaranteed Regions for Robust Model Interpretation on Continuous Data
proposals.motivation: As complex models like LLMs are deployed in high-stakes continuous domains (e.g., finance, healthcare), providing reliable and robust explanations is critical for trust and safety. Existing explanation methods like anchors or LIME often produce brittle, point-based rules that can be invalidated by small, realistic perturbations in the input, failing to characterize the model's stable behavior. This brittleness undermines user confidence and complicates reliable debugging and monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by identifying high-density, contiguous regions in the continuous feature space where a model's prediction is stable with high statistical probability, we can generate model explanations that are both robust to local perturbations and provide users with actionable, reliable guarantees.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that, for a given instance and prediction, uses adaptive sampling (inspired by batch active learning strategies) to explore the local feature space. It will fit a probabilistic model of the prediction boundary and iteratively expand a candidate region defined by soft constraints (e.g., ellipsoids or convex polytopes) until a statistical guarantee—calibrated via conformal prediction techniques—is met (e.g., 95% precision that the prediction holds within the region). The method will prioritize regions of high data density, assessed using pre-2026 atypicality measures or kernel density estimates, to ensure explanations are representative.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new class of explanation methods that provide explicit, statistical robustness guarantees for continuous data, moving beyond confidence scores to reliability in a region. It will deliver a practical tool for model developers and auditors to identify and communicate stable model behavior, directly addressing robustness concerns in human-in-the-loop evaluation frameworks like AlpacaFarm.
proposals.novelty: While existing anchors provide coverage guarantees for discrete data, and robustness research focuses on adversarial *vulnerabilities*, this method uniquely synthesizes them to produce *probabilistically guaranteed*, *density-aware* explanatory regions for continuous features, offering a proactive characterization of robustness rather than a vulnerability test.

proposals.title: Anchored Explanations: Interpretable Rule Extraction via Topological Manifold Partitioning
proposals.motivation: As LLMs and complex models are deployed in high-stakes settings, there is a critical need for interpretable, human-auditable rules that explain model behavior. Current methods like Anchors rely on discretization of continuous features, which can create lossy, arbitrary boundaries that misrepresent the true decision manifold. This proposal addresses the core challenge of generating faithful, geometric explanations directly in the continuous feature space, aligning with the need for reliable model understanding highlighted by pre-2026 work on atypicality and human feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a simplicial complex (e.g., a Delaunay triangulation or alpha shape) from strategically sampled points on the data manifold will yield a set of stable topological 'anchors.' These anchors can define convex, polyhedral regions in the continuous feature space that serve as precise, interpretable rules for model predictions without arbitrary discretization.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that first samples the feature space using techniques like strategic batch selection or influence functions to capture the manifold's structure. We will then construct a simplicial complex (e.g., using the Delaunay triangulation or a witness complex) over these samples. The vertices of the complex become the 'anchors.' For a given prediction, we will identify a minimal subset of anchors whose convex hull (or a topological neighborhood like a star) contains the instance and aligns with the model's decision boundary. The rule is defined as membership in this geometric region.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for generating geometrically faithful, interpretable rules from complex models, moving beyond axis-aligned, discretized explanations. It will provide a new tool for model auditing and debugging, particularly for LLMs, by offering explanations that preserve the continuous nature of the underlying data distribution. The resulting 'anchored' rules will be directly evaluable within human-in-the-loop frameworks like AlpacaFarm.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying computational topology (specifically, simplicial complex construction) to the problem of interpretable rule extraction, creating continuous, geometric explanations instead of discrete, binned rules. This bridges a gap between topological data analysis and practical explainable AI (XAI) for modern machine learning models.

proposals.title: StreamAnchor: Incremental, High-Fidelity Explanations for Black-Box Models in Non-Stationary Data Streams
proposals.motivation: The Anchor algorithm provides interpretable, high-precision explanations for model predictions but requires discrete features and static data, limiting its use in critical streaming applications like algorithmic trading or IoT monitoring where features are continuous and distributions drift. Current workarounds involve pre-processing discretization, which loses fidelity and creates unstable explanations over time, undermining trust in deployed AI systems. A method to maintain precise, continuous-space anchors incrementally is needed for reliable explanation in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by directly maintaining and efficiently updating sufficient statistics for conditional distributions in the raw feature space, we can construct a streaming Anchor algorithm that yields more faithful, stable, and performant explanations than discretization-based approaches in non-stationary contexts.
proposals.method: We will extend the Anchor framework with a core incremental sufficient statistics engine. For each candidate anchor (a set of feature intervals), we will maintain online aggregates (e.g., counts, means) of the model's prediction behavior conditioned on that anchor. Using techniques from adaptive streaming quantile estimation (like P² or KLL sketch), we will dynamically adjust interval boundaries as new data arrives to maintain high coverage and precision. The search for new anchors will be guided by a bandit-based strategy, prioritizing promising regions identified from recent data batches, ensuring computational efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) the first streaming, discretization-free algorithm for Anchor explanations, 2) a formal analysis of explanation stability and fidelity in non-stationary environments, and 3) an open-source system validated on real-world streaming benchmarks (e.g., financial time-series, sensor data) showing superior performance over static baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of high-precision, model-agnostic explanation with efficient streaming algorithms for continuous data, moving beyond static, discretized explanations to create a live explanation layer that co-evolves with the model and data stream.

proposals.title: Causal Anchors: Generating Faithful Explanations via Dependency-Aware Feature Block Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Anchor explanations are a leading method for interpretable, model-agnostic explanations, but they critically assume feature independence when generating perturbations. This assumption is routinely violated in real-world data with correlated or causally dependent features, leading to unrealistic synthetic data points and unstable, unfaithful explanations. Addressing this flaw is essential for deploying reliable explanation systems in high-stakes domains like healthcare and finance, where understanding model decisions on realistic data is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: By first inferring a sparse dependency or causal graph among features to define coherent 'feature blocks,' and then performing anchor generation via block-wise interventions, we can produce explanations that are significantly more faithful to the model's behavior and stable across different random seeds than standard anchor methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we adapt robust causal discovery methods (e.g., PC algorithm, NOTEARS) and high-dimensional conditional independence tests to learn a sparse dependency graph from the data or model's internal representations. Second, we design a novel block-wise anchor algorithm that treats densely connected subgraphs as atomic units for perturbation, using the graph to constrain the search space. The algorithm will employ a hybrid search strategy, combining the original anchor's beam search with graph-informed heuristics to efficiently identify high-coverage, low-cardinality blocks.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new, dependency-aware anchor generation algorithm with a publicly released codebase. It will provide the first formal analysis of explanation fidelity under feature dependencies and establish new evaluation metrics based on the plausibility of generated counterfactuals. The results will advance the field of interpretable machine learning by closing a key gap between explanation theory and practical data assumptions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted the feature independence issue, our approach is the first to explicitly integrate causal graph structure into the core anchor search mechanism, drawing a novel analogy from protein folding's contact-map constraint. This moves beyond simple covariance adjustments to a principled, intervention-based perturbation model.

proposals.title: Generative Adversarial Counterfactuals for Robust and Faithful Anchor Explanations
proposals.motivation: Anchor explanations provide interpretable 'if-then' rules for model predictions, but their reliability depends on sampling valid counterfactuals. For structured data like text, graphs, and time series, naive perturbation often creates unrealistic, out-of-distribution samples, leading to anchors that are either overly broad or miss key constraints. This invalid perturbation problem undermines the faithfulness and utility of explanations for complex models, including large language models (LLMs) whose behavior we aim to understand programmatically.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a domain-specific generative adversarial network (GAN) to produce only plausible, structure-respecting counterfactuals, we can generate anchor explanations that are significantly more precise, robust, and faithful to the underlying model's decision boundaries.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a generator, trained on the target data domain (e.g., text corpora, graph datasets), learns to produce minimal, realistic perturbations. This generator acts as an adversary to the anchor algorithm, which iteratively queries it for counterfactuals. The anchor algorithm (based on the 2018 Anchor framework) will then be optimized over this constrained, adversarial sample space. We will validate the approach on models trained for text classification (using LLMs like LLaMA), time-series forecasting, and graph property prediction, benchmarking against standard perturbation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel explanation system capable of producing reliable anchors for structured data, directly addressing the invalid perturbation problem. It will advance the field of interpretable AI by providing a rigorous, model-agnostic tool for debugging and understanding complex models, particularly in high-stakes domains where explanation fidelity is critical. The released framework will extend the evaluation paradigms from projects like AlpacaFarm to explanation robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses simple perturbations or VAEs for counterfactuals, our integration of an adversarial generator explicitly trained for the explanation task to constrain the search space is novel. This creates a direct, efficient co-optimization between explanation discovery and plausible sample generation, moving beyond confidence-based or typicality-based sampling.

proposals.title: Anchors for Sequences and Structures: A Framework for Faithful Explanations of LLMs and Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: As complex models like LLMs and GNNs are deployed on text, time-series, and graph data, there is a critical need for interpretable, faithful explanations of their predictions. Pre-2026, the anchor framework provides high-precision, if-then rules for tabular data, but lacks formulations for structured and sequential domains where perturbations must respect inherent data semantics and dependencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anchor framework can be generalized to structured data by defining semantically-grounded perturbation spaces and efficient candidate generation, producing concise, high-coverage explanations that are more faithful and actionable than post-hoc feature attribution methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the anchor algorithm by defining task-specific perturbation functions: for text, using masked language models and syntactic constraints; for graphs, employing graph editing operations (node/edge addition/deletion); for time series, using learned or frequency-based imputation. We will integrate these into an efficient beam-search or MCTS-based candidate generation process, leveraging the pre-2026 'Exploiting Programmatic Behavior of LLMs' work for efficient simulation, and evaluate precision/coverage on benchmarks from AlpacaFarm and graph classification tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first general-purpose anchor implementation for sequential and relational data, enabling rigorous, model-agnostic explanation for state-of-the-art models. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for reliability analysis, complementing work on 'atypicality,' and a foundation for training with explanation-based human feedback.
proposals.novelty: While anchors exist for tabular data, formulating them for structured spaces with complex constraints is novel. The integration of programmatic perturbation models with the anchor search algorithm to produce semantically valid explanations is a significant technical advance over prior feature-importance methods.

proposals.title: Anchors on a Budget: Co-Designing Sample Efficiency Guarantees and Lightweight Algorithms for On-Device Explainability
proposals.motivation: As complex models like LLMs are deployed on edge devices, there is a critical need for trustworthy, on-device explainability. Existing high-fidelity explanation methods, such as Anchors, are computationally expensive and sample-inefficient, making them impractical for resource-constrained environments. This creates a tension between explanation quality and deployment feasibility, hindering the adoption of transparent AI in low-resource settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By deriving new, tighter, and prescriptive sample complexity bounds for anchor explanations, we can co-design a lightweight algorithmic architecture that minimizes resource consumption while providing mathematically assured reliability guarantees.
proposals.method: We will first derive new PAC-style bounds for anchor sample complexity that explicitly incorporate algorithmic parameters (e.g., candidate rule set size, approximation tolerance). These bounds will then be inverted to prescriptively guide the design of a new anchor algorithm. This algorithm will feature adaptive sampling schedules, theory-guided pruning of the candidate rule space, and a dynamic tolerance mechanism, all optimized to meet a pre-specified resource budget (e.g., a maximum number of model queries) while guaranteeing explanation precision.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of sample-efficient anchor algorithms that are theoretically grounded and practically deployable on-device. It will provide a principled methodology for trading off explanation fidelity for computational cost, advancing the field of resource-aware explainable AI. The outcome will be a system where explanation reliability is mathematically assured within a strict resource budget.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses theory to analyze algorithms post-hoc, our approach uses prescriptive theoretical bounds to directly drive algorithmic design choices for minimal resource use. This creates a closed-loop, theory-guided system specifically for constrained environments, a significant advance over heuristic optimizations.

proposals.title: Amortized Decision Boundary Models for Sample-Efficient Anchor Generation and Robustness Validation
proposals.motivation: Explaining complex model predictions via anchors (sufficient conditions) is crucial for trust, but generating and validating anchors is computationally expensive, requiring many model queries. Current methods, like those in AlpacaFarm for learning from feedback, treat each anchor search independently, leading to redundant queries and poor scalability for validating model behavior across large feature spaces. This inefficiency hinders the practical application of rigorous robustness and interpretability audits, especially for large models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by amortizing the cost of learning a global, surrogate model of the classifier's decision boundary across multiple anchor searches, we can drastically reduce the sample complexity of anchor generation and validation compared to myopic, per-instance active search strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-active learning framework where a global probabilistic model (e.g., a Gaussian Process or a Bayesian Neural Network) approximates the decision boundary. A meta-acquisition function, inspired by principles of information gain and building on concepts from 'Beyond Confidence: Reliable Models Should Also Consider Atypicality', will guide perturbation selection. This function will balance refining the local anchor region against improving the global surrogate model. The system will be evaluated on benchmark tasks from AlpacaFarm and security datasets, measuring query efficiency and anchor fidelity against standard baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for sample-efficient model explanation and robustness auditing, directly reducing the cost of obtaining reliable anchors. It will contribute a new methodology for transferring knowledge across explanation tasks, advancing the scalability of interpretability methods. The resulting global boundary model can also serve as a reusable tool for analyzing model behavior and potential vulnerabilities, as explored in dual-use security contexts.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the shift from isolated, instance-specific anchor search to a globally amortized process that builds a transferable representation of model behavior. This moves beyond the standard active learning paradigm applied to single explanations by explicitly modeling and exploiting the shared structure of the decision surface across different data points.

proposals.title: ActiveAnchor: Efficient Anchor Validation via Query-by-Committee for High-Stakes LLM Explanations
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains like healthcare and finance, rigorous local explanations, such as anchors, are critical for trust and debugging. However, validating an anchor's precision requires numerous, expensive queries to the black-box model, which is computationally prohibitive for large models. Current methods lack a principled strategy to optimize this query budget, leading to inefficient or unreliable explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the Query-by-Committee (QBC) active learning paradigm to anchor search will strategically prioritize queries that resolve the greatest uncertainty among diverse interpretable surrogates, thereby achieving more precise anchors with fewer black-box model evaluations.
proposals.method: We will construct a committee of diverse, inherently interpretable models (e.g., shallow decision trees, rule lists, sparse linear models) trained to mimic the local behavior of the black-box LLM. For a candidate anchor, we generate a batch of perturbed instances and compute committee disagreement as the variance in predictions of whether the anchor holds. We will use a batch-mode QBC algorithm, inspired by BatchBALD, to select the top-k perturbations for actual LLM querying, updating the committee iteratively. The process continues until committee agreement converges or the query budget is exhausted.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, sample-efficient framework for generating high-precision anchor explanations, directly reducing the computational cost of explainability for large models. It will also advance the theory of active learning for explanation by formalizing the link between surrogate model diversity and explanation uncertainty quantification.
proposals.novelty: While QBC is classic in active learning, its application to optimize the validation phase of a specific explainability technique (anchors) is novel. The core innovation is framing anchor precision estimation as a committee disagreement problem, bridging efficient screening strategies from computational science with interpretable AI.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Explanations via Agnostic Discovery and Multi-Model Ensembling
proposals.motivation: Current methods for generating causal explanations or counterfactuals for complex models, like LLMs, often assume a known and correct causal graph, which is unrealistic in real-world settings. This fragility limits the reliability and trustworthiness of explanations, especially in high-stakes applications where understanding model behavior is critical. The need for robust, assumption-lean explanations aligns with the push for more reliable and interpretable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing causal discovery to learn an equivalence class of plausible data-generating structures and generating perturbations consistent across this class, we can produce causal explanations that are provably valid under a broad set of underlying models, thereby increasing their robustness and reliability.
proposals.method: We will first apply constraint-based (e.g., PC or FCI) and score-based (e.g., GES) causal discovery algorithms to observational data to learn a Markov equivalence class of Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs). We will then develop an ensembling algorithm that, for a given input, computes minimal perturbations that are valid (i.e., process-respecting) across all DAGs in the class. For computational tractability with large models, we will focus on robustly identified direct parent-child relationships, using techniques inspired by 'Beyond Confidence' to handle atypical regions of the input space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical toolkit for generating robust causal explanations without requiring a perfect causal model. It will advance the field of interpretable AI by making explanations more reliable under model uncertainty. The resulting methods will be directly applicable to explaining the behavior of complex models like those trained in AlpacaFarm.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its integration of agnostic causal discovery with robust perturbation generation for explanation, moving beyond single-graph reliance. It uniquely addresses the dual-use concern of generating reliable explanations even when the underlying data-generating process is only partially known.

proposals.title: Do-Explain: Causally Valid Counterfactual Explanations via Learned Structural Causal Models
proposals.motivation: Current methods for explaining black-box models, like LIME or SHAP, often rely on correlational feature importance, generating unrealistic perturbations that violate real-world causal dependencies. This limits their utility for actionable human decision-making, as they cannot answer valid 'what-if' questions. A causally grounded explanation framework is needed to ensure counterfactual instances are plausible and faithful to the data-generating process.
proposals.hypothesis: By learning a structural causal model (SCM) of the input features and using do-calculus to generate perturbations, we can produce counterfactual explanations that are more realistic, robust, and causally valid than those from standard feature attribution or gradient-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will learn or specify an SCM for the input domain, using causal discovery techniques (e.g., NOTEARS) or leveraging domain knowledge. Second, to explain a prediction, we will perform a targeted do-intervention on a feature, propagate the change through the SCM's structural equations to generate a valid counterfactual input, and observe the model's prediction shift. We will integrate this with instruction-tuned LLMs (like those in AlpacaFarm) to generate natural language explanations of the causal contrast, and evaluate explanation quality via human studies and atypicality measures from prior work on reliable models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for model explanation that moves from associative to causal reasoning, yielding more trustworthy and actionable explanations. It will also contribute a practical simulation framework, akin to AlpacaFarm but for causal what-if analysis, enabling systematic evaluation of explanation methods under known generative processes.
proposals.novelty: While causal models are used in epidemiology for intervention simulation, their direct application to generate valid input perturbations for explaining any trained ML model is novel. This approach fundamentally differs from existing explanation methods by enforcing causal constraints on the counterfactual generation process itself.

proposals.title: Causally-Constrained Perturbations for Valid and Faithful Explanations of LLM Behavior
proposals.motivation: Current methods for explaining LLM decisions, like feature attribution or counterfactual generation, often create unrealistic input perturbations that violate the underlying data-generating process. This leads to invalid explanations that misrepresent model causality and erode trust, especially in high-stakes applications. Grounding explanations in causal models of the data domain is a critical, unsolved challenge for reliable model auditing and human-AI collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: By generating input perturbations constrained by a causal graphical model of the data domain, we can produce more valid and faithful explanations of LLM behavior than those from standard, distribution-violating methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that integrates Structural Causal Models (SCMs) with explanation techniques. For a given input and LLM, we will learn or use a pre-specified SCM to define admissible interventions. We will then generate contrastive explanations by sampling from the interventional distribution (e.g., using do-calculus) rather than the observational distribution of arbitrary perturbations. This approach will be integrated into a batch evaluation pipeline, similar to AlpacaFarm, to systematically measure explanation quality via simulated human feedback on plausibility and usefulness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, causal framework for evaluating the validity of LLM explanations, moving beyond mere predictive confidence. It will yield a new benchmark and open-source tools for generating causally-consistent perturbations, directly advancing the reliability and trustworthiness of explainable AI for large language models.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference is used for model analysis, its direct application to constrain the *generation of explanatory perturbations* for LLMs is novel. This bridges the gap between high-level causal reasoning and practical, instance-level explanation methods.

proposals.title: Parsimonious Anchors: Synthesizing Minimal Explanations for LLM Decisions via Phylogenetic Optimization
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are deployed in high-stakes domains, providing human-interpretable explanations for their local decisions is critical for trust and debugging. Existing anchor-based explanation methods often produce complex, redundant rule sets that obscure the core logic. We need a principled approach to synthesize the simplest, most essential decision rules from observed model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing anchor synthesis as a maximum parsimony problem—searching for the symbolic program requiring the fewest evolutionary changes to explain local input-output pairs—will yield more concise, stable, and human-interpretable explanations than current heuristic or coverage-based methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the phylogenetic Maximum Parsimony algorithm for symbolic program search. The 'taxa' are input instances in a local neighborhood, their 'traits' are feature conditions, and the 'ancestral state' is the candidate anchor (a conjunction of conditions). The synthesis algorithm, integrated into a framework like AlpacaFarm, will perform a branch-and-bound search over the space of programmatic rules, minimizing a cost function that penalizes rule complexity and mismatches to the LLM's batch performance on perturbed inputs. Fidelity and atypicality metrics from prior work will guide the search and evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-inspired optimization framework for interpretable AI, producing a tool for generating minimal, stable anchors. It will advance the field by providing a formal, parsimony-based metric for explanation quality, moving beyond mere fidelity or coverage.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct analogy and technical adaptation of phylogenetic reconstruction algorithms—a well-established method in evolutionary biology—to the problem of synthesizing interpretable rules for machine learning models, a connection largely unexplored in pre-2026 ML interpretability research.

proposals.title: Mapping the Phase Space of Neural Networks: A Renormalization Group Framework for Universal Model Behavior
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) exhibit complex, emergent behaviors that are poorly understood and difficult to predict from their components. As models scale, we lack a principled framework to characterize their global operational regimes, vulnerabilities, and failure modes, hindering reliable deployment. Drawing an analogy to statistical physics, where the renormalization group (RG) successfully maps phase diagrams and universal scaling laws, offers a promising path to a more systematic theory of deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iterative coarse-graining of a neural network's computational graph will reveal scale-invariant 'fixed points'—stable functional motifs—and that the flow between these fixed points defines a phase diagram that predicts model capabilities and sensitivities across architectures, tasks, and data distributions.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational RG procedure for transformer-based LLMs. Starting from a trained model, we will define a coarse-graining operation that groups and abstracts sets of computational units (e.g., attention heads or MLP neurons) based on their functional similarity and interaction strength, using metrics from recent work on atypicality and programmatic behavior. Iteratively applying this operation will produce a flow in a space of effective computational 'theories.' We will analyze this flow to identify fixed points and critical exponents, correlating them with empirical performance, robustness, and vulnerability metrics from benchmarks and adversarial probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical and practical toolkit for predicting and explaining model behavior at a global scale. It will yield interpretable 'phase diagrams' for AI models, identifying universal scaling laws for error and robustness, and pinpointing architectural or data regimes prone to specific failures, directly informing safer and more efficient model training and evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes individual model components or specific emergent phenomena, this proposal is novel in adapting the full formal apparatus of the renormalization group—fixed points, flow, universality—to construct a global, predictive map of neural network behavior, moving beyond descriptive statistics to a theory of computational phases.

proposals.title: Discovering Global Behavioral Anchors: High-Precision Rule Extraction for Reliable LLM Prediction
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes settings, understanding their behavior is critical for safety and reliability. Current interpretability methods, like feature attribution or local explanations, fail to provide high-precision, human-understandable rules that generalize across broad input regions, leaving model behavior opaque at a global scale. This gap hinders rigorous auditing and the development of predictable, aligned AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by scaling the concept of 'anchors' (high-precision, if-then rules) from local explanations to a global model analysis, we can algorithmically discover a compact set of rules that accurately predict an LLM's output behavior—including failures and atypical responses—across large, semantically coherent regions of its input space.
proposals.method: We will extend the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to generate diverse, structured instruction-following batches for probing. Building on the 'atypicality' concept from prior work, we will treat rule discovery as a search problem over interpretable predicates (e.g., syntactic structures, semantic categories). We will develop a new algorithm that iteratively clusters model outputs, uses statistical constraint learning to propose candidate rules, and validates their precision and recall on held-out data, efficiently pruning the search space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable method for extracting global, human-interpretable rules that characterize LLM behavior. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for identifying systematic failure modes and biases, advancing beyond post-hoc explanation towards predictive behavioral modeling. The resulting 'anchor sets' could inform more robust training and red-teaming procedures.
proposals.novelty: While 'anchors' exist for local explainability, scaling them to find global, high-coverage behavioral rules for LLMs is unexplored. Our approach uniquely combines simulation-based probing, atypicality detection, and constraint learning to move from explaining single instances to characterizing model behavior programmatically.

proposals.title: Synthesizing Edge-Deployable Anchor Programs for On-Device Model Explanations
proposals.motivation: As machine learning models are increasingly deployed on resource-constrained edge devices, there is a critical need for explanations that are not only interpretable but also executable on the same hardware. Pre-2026 work on anchors provides local, rule-based explanations, but they are typically generated on powerful servers and are not designed for efficient execution. This creates a gap where the model's decision logic is embedded in a low-resource environment, but its explanation is not, hindering real-time, on-device interpretability and trust.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing anchor discovery as a resource-constrained program synthesis problem, we can generate minimal, executable explanation programs that are both faithful to the model's local decision boundary and efficient enough to run on the target edge device.
proposals.method: We will extend the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to generate synthetic feedback for explanation quality and efficiency. The core method adapts program synthesis techniques, guided by a multi-objective loss function that balances anchor precision/coverage with explicit resource constraints (e.g., operation count, memory footprint). We will leverage the programmatic behavior of LLMs, as studied in prior security work, to generate candidate explanation programs in a domain-specific language. These candidates will be iteratively refined via a batched optimization process that evaluates performance against a surrogate model, directly incorporating hardware-in-the-loop profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for generating directly deployable, resource-aware explanations, advancing the field of interpretable ML toward edge computing. It will provide a new benchmark for explanation efficiency and a methodology to co-design models and their explanations for constrained environments.
proposals.novelty: This is the first approach to unify the interpretability goal of anchors with the formal guarantees and hardware-awareness of program synthesis, explicitly optimizing explanations for on-device execution rather than just human readability.

proposals.title: Anchored Ensembles: Generating Faithful Explanations for Model Cascades via Human-Interpretable Decision Rules
proposals.motivation: As LLMs and other complex models are increasingly deployed in cascades or ensembles for improved performance and reliability, they become even more opaque. Pre-2026 explainability methods like LIME or SHAP are designed for single models and fail to account for the structured interactions and routing logic in multi-model systems, which is critical for trust and debugging in high-stakes applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the concept of 'anchors'—high-precision, human-interpretable rules—to the decision points and aggregation logic of model ensembles/cascades, we can generate more faithful and actionable explanations for the composite system's predictions than by explaining constituent models in isolation.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework, extending concepts from AlpacaFarm, to train and evaluate diverse ensembles (e.g., classifier cascades, LLM-router-expert systems). The core algorithm will adapt the Anchors explanation method to identify minimal sufficient sets of input features and, crucially, internal *routing decisions* (e.g., 'model B was consulted because model A's confidence was low') that guarantee a final prediction. We will use programmatic analysis of the system's control flow, informed by 'Beyond Confidence' insights on atypicality, to guide the search for these composite anchors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel explanation framework specifically for composite AI systems, 2) a public benchmark and simulation environment for evaluating explanations of ensembles, and 3) empirical evidence on how explanation fidelity impacts human ability to debug and improve cascaded models, closing the loop on learning from human feedback.
proposals.novelty: While anchors exist for single models, generating composite anchors that explicitly incorporate the gating and routing logic between models is a novel formulation. This moves beyond post-hoc feature attribution to explain the *system's decision-making process*, aligning with pre-2026 themes of exploiting programmatic behavior for understanding.

proposals.title: Boosting Beyond Trees: A Framework for Instruction-Tuned LLMs as Base Learners
proposals.motivation: Gradient boosting with additive regression trees (e.g., XGBoost) is a dominant paradigm for tabular and structured data, but its assumption of tree-based learners limits its applicability to complex, high-dimensional data like text and multimodal inputs. The rise of instruction-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs) offers powerful, flexible function approximators that could serve as superior base learners if integrated into a boosting framework, potentially unlocking new performance frontiers. This work challenges a core, long-standing design choice in boosting to bridge the gap between classical ensemble methods and modern foundation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a boosting meta-algorithm, using instruction-tuned LLMs as its base learners and guided by human preference feedback signals (like those in AlpacaFarm), can outperform both traditional tree-based boosting and standalone LLMs on complex prediction tasks requiring structured reasoning.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel boosting framework where each base learner is a 'lightweight' instruction-tuned LLM (e.g., a parameter-efficient fine-tuned version of a base model). The boosting algorithm will operate in rounds: 1) At each round, a new LLM base learner is instruction-tuned on a re-weighted dataset, where the 'labels' are the residual errors from the current ensemble, formulated as a natural language instruction-following task. 2) Human preference simulation (via AlpacaFarm) or atypicality measures will guide the selection and weighting of data points for the next round, moving beyond simple error magnitude. 3. The final model is an ensemble of these sequentially trained LLMs, whose outputs are aggregated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled framework for boosting with LLM base learners, expanding the theoretical and practical scope of ensemble methods. It will yield a new state-of-the-art method for tasks requiring nuanced language understanding and structured prediction, demonstrated on benchmarks for complex textual inference and programmatic behavior analysis. The research will also offer insights into the 'weak learner' requirements for boosting in high-capacity model regimes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly replacing additive regression trees with instruction-tuned LLMs within a boosting loop, a non-trivial integration due to the non-additive, instruction-conditioned nature of LLMs. It uniquely leverages human feedback simulation and atypicality concepts from the researcher's prior work to steer the boosting process, rather than relying solely on gradient-based weighting.

proposals.title: First-Order Gradient Boosting: Scalable Ensemble Learning Without Hessian Statistics
proposals.motivation: Gradient boosting is a cornerstone of machine learning, but its dominant implementations (e.g., XGBoost, LightGBM) rely on second-order gradient statistics (Hessians) for optimal tree structure learning. This dependency creates computational and memory bottlenecks, especially for massive datasets and high-dimensional parameter spaces common in modern LLM fine-tuning and large-scale batch learning tasks. A method requiring only first-order gradients would enable more efficient and scalable training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a boosting algorithm can be designed to match the convergence rate and performance of second-order methods using only first-order gradient information, by strategically incorporating variance reduction techniques and adaptive step-size selection derived from the gradient history.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel boosting framework, FOGBoost, that adapts the functional gradient descent perspective. Instead of approximating the Newton step, we will use a momentum-based gradient aggregation scheme, inspired by stochastic optimization (e.g., Adam), to estimate adaptive learning rates for each leaf node. We will integrate a control variates technique, similar to SVRG, to reduce the variance of gradient estimates across boosting iterations, ensuring stable updates. The tree structure will be learned by optimizing a first-order-only split criterion derived from the gradient directional variance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, highly scalable boosting algorithm that reduces per-iteration computational cost and memory footprint, making large-scale ensemble training more accessible. It will extend the theoretical understanding of first-order optimization in the boosting context. The method will be validated on large-scale instruction-following fine-tuning tasks, demonstrating efficiency gains over Hessian-based boosters.
proposals.novelty: While first-order methods exist for neural networks, applying variance-reduced first-order optimization to the specific structure of gradient boosting trees, entirely replacing the canonical second-order split gain formula, is a novel and non-obvious direction. This bridges ideas from stochastic optimization with ensemble learning in a new way.

proposals.title: AdaBoost-LLM: Automated Hyperparameter Adaptation for Boosting via Learned Meta-Gradients
proposals.motivation: Boosting algorithms like AdaBoost are foundational for creating robust ensembles from weak learners, but their performance is highly sensitive to hyperparameters like the learning rate. Manual tuning is costly, and static schedules are suboptimal across diverse datasets. This problem is amplified when using modern, complex base learners (e.g., small LLMs or large neural networks), where each boosting iteration is computationally expensive, making efficient, automated adaptation crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight meta-learner, trained via gradient-based optimization on a distribution of simulated learning tasks, can learn to dynamically adjust the boosting learning rate and other parameters (e.g., instance re-weighting schemes) more effectively than fixed schedules or rule-based adapters, leading to faster convergence and better final performance.
proposals.method: We will frame hyperparameter adaptation as a meta-learning problem. Inspired by AlpacaFarm's simulation framework, we will create a diverse set of synthetic and real-world tabular/text classification tasks. A small recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer meta-controller will observe the trajectory of training losses, validation metrics, and model confidence (inspired by 'Beyond Confidence' atypicality measures) across boosting iterations. This controller will output per-iteration adjustments. We will train it using a performance-predictive loss via truncated backpropagation through time, leveraging efficient batch updates to simulate many boosting runs in parallel.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a general, plug-and-play method for adaptive boosting that reduces the need for expert tuning. It will provide new insights into the coupling between base learner characteristics (e.g., LLM instruction-following capability) and optimal ensemble dynamics. We will release an open-source framework integrating with popular boosting libraries.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning for hyperparameter optimization exists, its application to the sequential, non-stationary process of boosting—particularly with a focus on leveraging programmatic behavior and confidence signals from modern base learners—is novel. This moves beyond simple learning rate decay to a context-aware policy.

proposals.title: Test-Driven Robustness: Inverting Evaluation Suites for Adversarial Fine-Tuning of Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current methods for evaluating LLM robustness, such as adversarial test suites, primarily serve as post-hoc diagnostics. This creates a gap: identifying failure modes does not directly translate to fixing them. Given the critical need for reliable, secure models that resist manipulation and perform consistently, we need methods that can systematically convert these diagnostic tests into training data to improve model resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iterative, closed-loop process of generating adversarial examples from a formal test suite, using them for fine-tuning, and then re-evaluating the model on an expanded suite will produce LLMs that are significantly more robust to the failure modes captured by the suite, without catastrophic forgetting of general instruction-following capabilities.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated framework that treats a test suite (e.g., for security, truthfulness, or instruction following) as a specification. Using methods inspired by programmatic behavior exploitation and data distillation, we will convert test logic and failure cases into synthetic training examples. These will be used in a batch-based adversarial fine-tuning loop, leveraging human feedback simulation (like AlpacaFarm) to maintain helpfulness. We will explicitly monitor atypicality metrics to ensure the training distribution does not become overly narrow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology for turning static evaluation benchmarks into dynamic robustness engines. It will contribute a new fine-tuning paradigm, 'test-driven robustness,' and an associated open-source framework. The resulting models will demonstrate measurable gains in robustness on held-out adversarial challenges while preserving standard performance.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is well-known, the key novelty is the systematic inversion of a comprehensive, multi-faceted test suite—not just a single attack algorithm—into a curriculum for training. This moves beyond using tests for evaluation to using them as core, generative components of the training process itself.

proposals.title: Behavioral Stress Testing for Training Data Audits: Identifying Shortcomings via Programmatic LLM Probes
proposals.motivation: Modern LLMs are trained on massive, often opaque datasets, making it difficult to identify systematic gaps, biases, or quality issues that lead to unreliable or unsafe model behavior. Current data auditing methods are largely static, focusing on dataset statistics or manual inspection, which fails to capture how data shortcomings manifest dynamically during model inference. This gap is critical, as evidenced by research on adversarial attacks exploiting learned behaviors and the challenges of learning from human feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying behavioral test concepts—specifically, constructing targeted, programmatic instruction batches that probe for failure modes—we can systematically audit and identify latent shortcomings in LLM training datasets, such as missing knowledge, bias patterns, or contradictory examples.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that generates diverse, adversarial instruction batches designed to stress-test specific behavioral dimensions (e.g., reasoning consistency, factual grounding, safety). Inspired by AlpacaFarm and security attack research, we will use a seed LLM to automate probe generation. These probes will be executed on a model trained on the target dataset, and its responses will be analyzed using metrics from reliable prediction research, like atypicality and confidence calibration. The failure patterns will be algorithmically traced back to likely deficiencies in the training data composition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, automated methodology for proactive training data auditing, moving beyond post-hoc model evaluation. It will contribute a framework and benchmark for 'data stress tests' that can help dataset creators identify and mitigate flaws before model training, improving data-centric AI development.
proposals.novelty: While behavioral testing exists for models, applying it as a direct diagnostic tool for the training dataset itself is novel. The approach uniquely combines programmatic adversarial probe generation from security research with reliability metrics to create a closed-loop data audit system.

proposals.title: Memory-Efficient Training for Complex, Non-Linear Computation Graphs via Selective Gradient Checkpointing and Dynamic Subgraph Caching
proposals.motivation: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and other deep networks incorporate more complex, non-linear structures (e.g., mixture-of-experts, graph neural networks with dynamic topologies, or models with extensive programmatic branching), their memory footprint during training becomes a critical bottleneck. Pre-2026 memory-efficient methods like gradient checkpointing are often designed for linear or simple feedforward graphs, struggling with irregular, data-dependent computation paths. This limits the scale and architectural innovation of models that can be practically trained.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training algorithm combining a learned, performance-aware policy for selective checkpointing with a dynamic subgraph caching mechanism can drastically reduce peak memory usage for complex, non-linear computation graphs without significantly compromising training throughput.
proposals.method: We will extend the core algorithm from 'AlpacaFarm' and 'Beyond Confidence' by treating the training process as a reinforcement learning problem where the agent decides which intermediate activations to checkpoint or recompute within a complex graph. The policy will be trained using a reward function balancing memory savings and recomputation cost, informed by performance metrics from historical training batches. For highly repetitive non-linear sub-structures (e.g., recurrent control flows in LLMs following instructions), we will implement a dynamic cache that stores and reuses forward pass results based on a learned signature of the subgraph's input, validated by techniques from 'Exploiting Programmatic Behavior of LLMs'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source framework for training larger and more architecturally innovative models on existing hardware. It will contribute a formal analysis of the memory-time trade-off for non-linear graphs and demonstrate empirical gains on benchmarks like training mixture-of-experts LLMs and graph neural networks over complex relational data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static checkpointing heuristics, our method learns an adaptive policy optimized for specific graph structures and batch characteristics. The integration of subgraph caching for non-linear, data-dependent computations moves beyond prior work focused primarily on linearized graphs.

proposals.title: Sublinear Memory Scaling for Large-Batch, Distributed LLM Training via Integrated Model, Data, and Gradient Checkpointing Parallelism
proposals.motivation: Training state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) is bottlenecked by GPU memory, limiting batch size and model scale. While sublinear memory algorithms like gradient checkpointing exist, and parallelism strategies (data/model) are used, they are often optimized separately. An integrated framework is needed to maximize training throughput on multi-GPU clusters by co-designing memory-saving algorithms with parallelism, a critical need pre-2026 as models continue to grow.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly optimizing the scheduling of gradient checkpointing (sublinear memory) with the partitioning strategies of model and data parallelism, we can achieve near-linear scaling in training throughput across hundreds of GPUs while maintaining stable optimization for very large effective batch sizes.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation and optimization framework extending concepts from AlpacaFarm. The core is a cost model that incorporates memory footprints from selective activation rematerialization (checkpointing) and communication overheads from 3D parallelism (pipeline, tensor, data). Using this model, we will design a search algorithm to find the optimal parallel configuration and checkpoint schedule that minimizes per-iteration time for a given cluster and model. We will validate the system by training medium-scale LLMs (e.g., 10B+ parameters) on multi-node GPU clusters, measuring throughput and final task performance (e.g., instruction following).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified system for memory-efficient, large-scale LLM training, delivering open-source software and heuristics for optimal configuration. It will contribute empirical evidence on the optimization landscape of very large-batch training facilitated by integrated memory management, advancing the practical scalability of LLM training pipelines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight co-optimization of a sublinear memory algorithm's internal scheduling with the external parallelism strategy, moving beyond their independent application. This treats the memory budget as a schedulable resource across the entire distributed system, not just a single GPU.

proposals.title: Memory-Efficient RL Training via Gradient Checkpointing and Selective Experience Replay
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement learning (RL) with deep networks, especially in complex environments or with large-scale models, is severely bottlenecked by GPU memory. This limits the complexity of policies that can be trained, the batch sizes for stable learning, and the length of environmental trajectories that can be processed. Pre-2026, memory reduction techniques like gradient checkpointing are standard in supervised learning but underexplored for the unique, sequential, and replay-buffer-dependent workflows of RL.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically applying and adapting memory-saving techniques from supervised learning—specifically gradient checkpointing and compressed experience storage—to RL training pipelines will enable the training of deeper, more capable agents on existing hardware without sacrificing sample efficiency or final performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt gradient checkpointing to the temporal computation graphs of on-policy (e.g., PPO) and off-policy (e.g., SAC) RL algorithms, selectively storing only critical states. We will integrate this with a method for compressing and selectively retaining experiences in the replay buffer based on 'atypicality' metrics (inspired by Guestrin's prior work) to reduce memory overhead. The training framework will be implemented and evaluated within an extended version of the AlpacaFarm simulation environment, adapted for RL tasks, to measure performance, memory footprint, and training dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source framework for memory-efficient deep RL training. It will deliver empirical benchmarks showing how memory optimization affects convergence and final policy quality in complex environments. The research will also yield insights into which experiences are most valuable to retain when storage is constrained, bridging memory optimization and RL theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design and synergistic application of checkpointing and intelligent experience replay specifically for RL's iterative training loop, moving beyond isolated supervised learning techniques. Furthermore, using atypicality to guide memory allocation in the replay buffer is a novel intersection of reliable modeling and systems efficiency for RL.

proposals.title: The Parameter-Performance Pareto: A Unified Framework for Pruning and Low-Rank Adaptation in Instruction-Tuned LLMs
proposals.motivation: The scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has created a critical memory bottleneck, both for storing billions of parameters and for performing inference. Pre-2026, methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) reduce task-specific memory for fine-tuning, while pruning reduces inference-time memory, but these are treated as separate trade-offs. A unified framework is needed to explicitly navigate the joint trade-off between total parameter memory, computational performance, and instruction-following capability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Pareto-optimal frontier exists for instruction-tuned LLMs, where combinations of structured pruning and low-rank adaptation can achieve comparable task performance to dense models with significantly reduced total parameter storage.
proposals.method: We will extend the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to evaluate the joint parameter-performance trade-off. For a given base model (e.g., LLaMA), we will apply iterative magnitude pruning to create a spectrum of sparsely activated sub-networks. Concurrently, we will train LoRA adapters of varying ranks on these pruned bases for instruction-following tasks. We will analyze the resulting 3D Pareto surface (density vs. adapter rank vs. performance) using metrics from 'Beyond Confidence' to assess reliable atypicality of pruned models. The core algorithm will automate the search for optimal (density, rank) pairs under a fixed memory budget.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated method for constructing memory-efficient LLMs tailored to specific hardware constraints, moving beyond isolated compression techniques. It will yield a public benchmark within AlpacaFarm for evaluating the parameter-performance Pareto frontier and offer new insights into the interplay between model sparsity and efficient adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While pruning and LoRA are established, jointly optimizing them as a unified memory-performance trade-off for instruction-tuned models is novel. This approach directly links the 'programmatic behavior' exploited in dual-use attacks to the structural components of the model that are most critical for retaining instruction-following ability after compression.

proposals.title: Precision-First MCMC: Adapting Stochastic Gradient HMC for High-Certainty, Low-Data Inference with LLM Guidance
proposals.motivation: Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (SGHMC) excels in large-scale, noisy-gradient settings but is inefficient for small-data, high-precision tasks where gradient noise is minimal and computational budgets are constrained. This mismatch is critical for reliable, calibrated inference in domains like scientific modeling or safety-critical decision systems, where data is scarce but certainty is paramount. Recent work on LLM atypicality and reliability underscores the need for robust, sample-efficient Bayesian methods to complement modern large models.
proposals.hypothesis: By re-parameterizing SGHMC's friction term to dominate the now-small stochastic noise and integrating a pre-trained LLM as a structured prior or proposal generator, we can achieve faster convergence and more accurate posterior estimation in low-data, high-precision regimes compared to standard SGHMC and HMC.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified SGHMC algorithm where the friction coefficient is dynamically tuned based on a real-time estimate of gradient noise variance, forcing a diffusion-dominated dynamics suitable for small noise. For initialization and proposal generation in complex, low-data spaces, we will leverage a frozen, instruction-tuned LLM (e.g., from the AlpacaFarm lineage) conditioned on the data to output a structured prior distribution or promising parameter regions. This LLM-guided warm-start will be integrated into the MCMC chain, with performance evaluated on calibrated Bayesian logistic regression and small-scale neural network inference tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, efficient MCMC variant tailored for the small-data, high-certainty setting, bridging the gap between scalable stochastic sampling and precise inference. It will provide a novel framework for using pre-trained LLMs as informative priors within Bayesian computation, advancing the goal of reliable, human-aligned model uncertainty quantification.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the co-adaptation of a foundational sampling algorithm (SGHMC) for a regime it was not designed for, coupled with the non-obvious use of an instruction-following LLM not as a predictor, but as a computationally intelligent prior for guiding Bayesian inference in data-scarce environments.

proposals.title: Analyzing Stochastic Gradient MCMC for Multi-Modal Posteriors: Atypicality, Mode Coverage, and LLM Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (SGHMC) is a key tool for scalable Bayesian inference in large models, but its theoretical guarantees are largely limited to log-concave or uni-modal target distributions. Modern machine learning models, especially large neural networks and LLMs fine-tuned with human feedback, have complex, multi-modal loss landscapes and posteriors. Understanding SGHMC's performance on these distributions is critical for reliable uncertainty quantification in high-stakes applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a measure of atypicality—quantifying how 'surprising' a sample is relative to training data—into the SGHMC convergence analysis, we can derive novel, non-asymptotic bounds on its mode discovery and mixing rates for a class of non-convex, multi-modal distributions.
proposals.method: We will extend the theoretical framework of SGHMC by analyzing its dynamics as a perturbed underdamped Langevin diffusion. The core technical approach is to define and incorporate an atypicality score, inspired by pre-2026 out-of-distribution detection methods, into the potential function. We will then use Lyapunov function techniques and Foster-Lyapunov drift conditions to prove convergence bounds, explicitly relating mixing time to the number and separation of modes. Empirical validation will be conducted on synthetic multi-modal distributions and on the posterior of LLMs fine-tuned using human feedback simulators like AlpacaFarm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for SGHMC on a rigorous class of non-convex, multi-modal targets, directly addressing a major gap in scalable Bayesian deep learning theory.
