It will also yield practical insights for tuning SGHMC in LLM fine-tuning, where capturing diverse behavioral modes (e.g., from human preference data) is essential for robust and reliable model deployment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in bridging the theory of stochastic gradient MCMC with the concept of atypicality from reliable prediction, creating a new analytical tool to handle multi-modality, rather than relying on restrictive convexity assumptions common in prior analyses.

proposals.title: Scalable Bayesian Uncertainty for Human-in-the-Loop Systems: Applying SGHMC to Large-Scale Instruction-Tuned LLMs
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly fine-tuned via human feedback (e.g., RLHF, instruction following) for real-world tasks, quantifying their predictive uncertainty is critical for safety and reliability. Pre-2026, scalable Bayesian inference for such massive, non-convex models remains a significant challenge, with Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (SGHMC) primarily validated on neural networks and matrix factorization. Extending these methods to the complex, human-aligned models central to modern AI is an urgent open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SGHMC can be effectively adapted to generate meaningful posterior samples for large-scale, instruction-tuned LLMs, providing superior uncertainty quantification compared to standard point estimates or simpler Bayesian approximations, without prohibitive computational overhead.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SGHMC algorithm to the training dynamics of instruction-following LLMs (e.g., models like Alpaca). The core technical challenge is designing efficient gradient estimators for the loss landscapes defined by human feedback datasets and batch training procedures. We will integrate SGHMC into the fine-tuning loop, leveraging frameworks like AlpacaFarm for simulation, and evaluate on benchmarks measuring calibration, atypicality detection (inspired by 'Beyond Confidence'), and robustness to adversarial prompts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable Bayesian inference tool for the next generation of human-aligned AI models, directly contributing to their reliability and safety. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and value of full-batch Bayesian methods for LLMs, moving beyond point estimates to richer uncertainty representations.
proposals.novelty: While SGHMC is known, its application to the specific architecture and training paradigm of instruction-tuned LLMs fine-tuned with human feedback is novel and non-trivial, bridging scalable Bayesian methods with the forefront of human-in-the-loop learning systems.

proposals.title: Learning with Eventual Consistency: Relaxing Synchronization Requirements in Distributed Training and Human Feedback Loops
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale ML systems, from distributed neural network training to iterative learning from human feedback (as in AlpacaFarm), rely on strong consistency models (e.g., synchronous parameter updates) which create significant communication bottlenecks and latency. This limits scalability and real-time adaptation. The core question is whether many learning algorithms truly require this strict, expensive consistency to converge to performant models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that many iterative ML algorithms—including those for training LLMs and learning from human feedback—are robust to eventual or partial consistency in their underlying computational graph, allowing for substantial gains in efficiency and scalability with minimal impact on final model performance and reliability.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework extending concepts from AlpacaFarm to test consistency-relaxed learning. For distributed training, we will adapt batch SGD and Adam to operate under asynchronous, stochastic communication schedules between workers, measuring performance degradation. For human feedback loops, we will simulate instruction-following training where feedback integration is delayed or batched non-uniformly. We will analyze the conditions (e.g., gradient staleness bounds, feedback latency) under which algorithms remain stable, leveraging atypicality measures from prior work to detect when inconsistency leads to unreliable predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for designing more efficient, scalable ML systems by formally characterizing the consistency-accuracy-efficiency trade-off. It will deliver concrete guidelines and modified algorithms for distributed training and online learning from feedback that reduce synchronization overhead while maintaining model reliability.
proposals.novelty: While eventual consistency is studied in databases, its systematic application to core ML training loops and human-in-the-loop learning is unexplored. The novelty lies in directly challenging a fundamental systems assumption in ML—the need for a strongly consistent computational graph—and quantifying its necessity.

proposals.title: LIME-Tab: Faithful and Human-Interpretable Explanations for Tabular Model Predictions
proposals.motivation: As complex models like gradient-boosted trees and deep tabular networks are increasingly deployed in high-stakes structured data domains (e.g., finance, healthcare), understanding their predictions is critical for trust and debugging. While LIME is a seminal model-agnostic explanation framework, its standard implementations are optimized for text and images, lacking principled adaptations for the heterogeneous, mixed-type, and often sparse or correlated features inherent in real-world tabular data. This gap limits the reliability and actionability of explanations for practitioners.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a LIME variant with a perturbation mechanism and similarity kernel specifically tailored for tabular data structures, we can generate more faithful and human-interpretable local explanations than those produced by directly applying standard LIME.
proposals.method: We will extend the LIME framework by developing a data-type-aware perturbation function that samples plausible synthetic instances respecting feature distributions and constraints (e.g., categorical one-hots, ordinal relationships). We will design a novel similarity kernel that incorporates feature importance from a fast, interpretable surrogate model (like a shallow tree) to weight perturbations, moving beyond simple Euclidean distance. The method will be evaluated using the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to efficiently collect human feedback on explanation quality and usability, iteratively refining the approach based on metrics of instruction following and user preference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, open-source tool for explaining any black-box model on tabular data, directly addressing a pressing need in applied machine learning. It will contribute a systematic evaluation, using human-in-the-loop simulation, of what constitutes a 'good' explanation for structured data, moving beyond automated fidelity metrics to measure practical utility.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies LIME off-the-shelf to tabular data, our core novelty is the co-design of the perturbation and weighting mechanisms with the data's intrinsic structure and the integration of human feedback simulation for optimization, ensuring explanations are both statistically faithful and useful to end-users.

proposals.title: Scalable Submodular Pick for Interpretability: Batch-Aware Selection from Large Datasets and Model Classes
proposals.motivation: The submodular pick step in interpretability frameworks like LIME is crucial for selecting a small, diverse set of instances to explain a model's global behavior. However, existing algorithms do not scale to the massive datasets and diverse model classes (e.g., large ensembles, instruction-tuned LLMs) prevalent in pre-2026 machine learning. This bottleneck limits the practical application of post-hoc interpretability to real-world, large-scale systems where understanding model decisions is essential for trust and debugging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by reformulating submodular pick as a batch-aware, streaming optimization problem and leveraging pre-trained embeddings for efficient similarity computation, we can develop algorithms that scale near-linearly with dataset size while preserving explanation fidelity and diversity.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will use pre-trained LLM embeddings (e.g., from the Alpaca models) to project data points into a semantic space, enabling fast, approximate similarity calculations for coverage functions. Second, we will design a novel batch-greedy optimization procedure, inspired by mini-batch training, that processes data in large chunks to maximize a submodular coverage objective under a streaming data constraint. We will integrate atypicality scores (from Guestrin's prior work) as a weighting factor to ensure the selection includes challenging, non-prototypical examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable, open-source framework for submodular pick capable of handling datasets with millions of instances and complex model outputs. It will directly enable more comprehensive interpretability audits of large models trained with human feedback, as studied in AlpacaFarm. The algorithms will be evaluated on both standard benchmarks and massive text datasets processed by instruction-following LLMs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of batch-efficient submodular optimization with semantic embeddings from modern LLMs specifically for interpretability selection, and the explicit incorporation of atypicality to go beyond simple representativeness, addressing a key scaling challenge ignored in prior interpretability research.

proposals.title: LIME-Driven Automated Model Correction via Instruction-Tuning with Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes settings, post-hoc explanations like LIME are used for debugging but rarely for direct correction, creating a costly manual loop. Automating correction using explanations could enable continuous, low-cost model improvement, especially for edge cases identified as atypical or unreliable. This aligns with the need for reliable, self-improving systems that learn from human feedback, as explored in AlpacaFarm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic discrepancies between a model's predictions and its LIME-generated local explanations can be used to synthesize targeted correction data, and that instruction-tuning LLMs on this data will yield measurable improvements in model reliability and performance on previously faulty examples.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that, for a given faulty prediction, uses LIME to identify the most influential input features. A rule-based or LLM-based synthesizer will generate corrected output instructions based on these features. These (input, explanation, corrected output) triplets will form a synthetic dataset for instruction-tuning the original model. We will test this on benchmarks where models exhibit programmatic failures or overconfidence, evaluating correction success and generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated method for model self-correction that bridges explainability and training. It will provide a framework for using interpretability tools not just for audit but for repair, advancing towards more reliable and maintainable AI systems. It extends research on learning from feedback by automating the data generation step.
proposals.novelty: While LIME is used for insight, its direct, automated conversion into corrective training data is novel. This moves beyond using explanations for human-in-the-loop debugging to a closed-loop system, combining interpretability, dataset synthesis, and instruction-tuning in a new way.

proposals.title: BatchLIME: Efficient, Instruction-Guided Local Explanations for Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains, understanding their predictions is critical for trust and safety. However, popular local explanation methods like LIME are computationally prohibitive for massive models, requiring thousands of forward passes per explanation. This bottleneck prevents the scalable, real-time interpretability needed for monitoring and improving these systems based on human feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by exploiting the programmatic, instruction-following behavior of LLMs and batching perturbation-based queries, we can generate faithful local explanations with orders-of-magnitude fewer model calls, without sacrificing explanation quality.
proposals.method: We will develop BatchLIME, a framework that reformulates LIME's perturbation sampling as an instruction-following task for the target LLM. Instead of many individual queries, we construct a single, structured prompt containing a batch of perturbed inputs and an instruction to output scores for all. We will integrate this with an efficient approximation of the LIME regression step, using influence functions estimated from a small set of atypicality-weighted training examples to identify critical model behaviors, reducing the required perturbation count.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical tool for explaining predictions from models with hundreds of billions of parameters in near real-time. It will advance the field of interpretable AI by making local explanations feasible for production-scale LLMs, directly supporting the development of reliable systems that learn from human feedback.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is treating explanation generation as a batch-inference, instruction-following problem for the model being explained, a dual-use of its own capabilities. This contrasts with prior work that treats the model as a black-box function to be probed externally.

proposals.title: Minimal Explanatory Sets for Efficient Model Debugging
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are deployed in high-stakes settings, identifying specific flaws or biases is critical. However, existing explanation methods (e.g., feature attributions, counterfactuals) can produce overwhelming, redundant, or unactionable outputs, hindering efficient debugging. A systematic approach to find the smallest, most informative set of explanations would drastically reduce the human cost of diagnosing and correcting model failures.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given model flaw, there exists a minimal set of human-interpretable explanations (e.g., contrastive examples, concept activations) that is both necessary and sufficient for a human expert to reliably identify the underlying failure mode.
proposals.method: We will frame the search for a minimal explanation set as a subset selection problem over a large candidate pool generated via methods like influence functions, RICE, and programmatic adversarial prompts. Inspired by Carlos Guestrin's work on data subset selection and active learning, we will develop an iterative, human-in-the-loop algorithm. This algorithm will use a learned utility function, trained via preference learning from human feedback (similar to AlpacaFarm), to sequentially select the explanation that maximizes expected information gain about the flaw while minimizing set size and cognitive load.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical tool for efficient model debugging by identifying concise, actionable explanation sets. It will advance the field of interpretable ML beyond generating individual explanations towards optimizing explanatory sequences for human understanding and intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates explanations, our approach uniquely optimizes for minimality and sufficiency of an explanation *set* for a specific debugging task, directly integrating programmatic flaw discovery with human feedback loops to learn what constitutes a diagnostically useful explanation.

proposals.title: Dense Explanations for Human-AI Alignment: A Utility-Driven Evaluation in Instruction Following
proposals.motivation: Current research on explainable AI (XAI) heavily favors sparse, feature-attribution explanations (e.g., SHAP, LIME), which may not align with how humans build trust or learn from complex models. In high-stakes domains like medicine or finance, users often require comprehensive context, not just a few salient features, to understand model rationale and potential failure modes. This creates a critical gap in aligning LLM explanations with human learning processes for effective instruction following and oversight.
proposals.hypothesis: For complex, multi-faceted tasks where LLMs are deployed, dense explanations—providing a holistic, non-sparse account of model reasoning—will lead to higher human task performance, better error detection, and increased calibrated trust compared to traditional sparse explanations.
proposals.method: We will build on the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to conduct large-scale, controlled human-subject studies. We will fine-tune instruction-following LLMs (e.g., variants of Llama-2) to generate both sparse (via post-hoc methods like Integrated Gradients on retrieved contexts) and dense (via chain-of-thought or natural language rationales) explanations for their outputs on benchmark tasks. We will measure human performance (task success, time), reliability assessment (inspired by 'Beyond Confidence' work on atypicality), and adversarial robustness (linking to 'Exploiting Programmatic Behavior') in a batch evaluation setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, empirical evidence for when and why dense explanations are superior, moving the field beyond a default sparsity prior. It will deliver a novel evaluation framework within AlpacaFarm for explanation utility and contribute new fine-tuning strategies for generating task-optimal explanatory outputs.
proposals.novelty: It challenges the dominant sparsity paradigm in XAI by directly testing the assumption that 'less is more' for human-AI collaboration, and it uniquely integrates Guestrin's themes of human learning, set-based evaluation, and reliable model assessment into the study of explanations.

proposals.title: Personalized Explanation Generation via Expertise-Aware Preference Optimization
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to explain model predictions (e.g., in healthcare or finance), a one-size-fits-all explanation often fails. Pre-2026, methods like instruction-following and learning from human feedback (e.g., RLHF) optimize for a generic "helpful" assistant, not for adapting explanations to a user's specific expertise level and immediate goal, which limits practical utility and trust.
proposals.hypothesis: An LLM fine-tuned with a multi-objective, expertise-conditioned reward model can generate explanations that are simultaneously more satisfying and more efficient (e.g., faster comprehension, better downstream decisions) for users of different expertise levels than a single, generic explanation model.
proposals.method: We will extend the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to model users with discrete expertise levels (novice, intermediate, expert) and goals (e.g., understand cause, apply fix). We will train a single LLM to generate personalized explanations using a novel batch training procedure with Multi-Objective Reward Conditioning (MORC), where rewards from multiple simulated user models (trained via behavior cloning on collected human feedback per expertise group) are combined. The model's instruction prefix will explicitly condition on the user's expertise and goal.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel training paradigm for generating personalized, context-aware explanations from a single model. 2) A simulated evaluation framework, extending AlpacaFarm, for benchmarking explanation personalization. 3) Empirical evidence on how explanation features (e.g., detail, jargon) should vary with user expertise and task goal.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on atypicality and reliable models considers when a model is uncertain, we explicitly optimize the *communication* of that uncertainty and reasoning for a human audience. This moves beyond standard instruction-following by making the user's cognitive state a first-class conditioning variable in the training objective.

proposals.title: SET-XAI: Scalable Explanation Trees for Complex Data via Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
proposals.motivation: Model-agnostic interpretability methods like LIME and SHAP are foundational for tabular data but struggle with the high-dimensional, structured semantics of text and relational data. As LLMs and complex models are increasingly deployed on these data types, there is a critical need for explanations that are both faithful to the model and comprehensible to human users for debugging and trust. This gap limits the reliable application of AI in high-stakes domains.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating perturbation-based feature attribution with a human-in-the-loop feedback mechanism, we can generate structured, hierarchical explanation summaries (explanation sets) for text and structured data that are more faithful and useful than standard linear approximations.
proposals.method: We will extend the concept of 'sufficient explanations' (sets of features) to complex data by defining semantic perturbation operators for text (e.g., span replacement via LLMs) and structured records (e.g., attribute masking). Using a multi-armed bandit framework informed by the AlpacaFarm simulation, we will efficiently query a simulated human preference model to iteratively refine candidate explanation sets. The final output will be a sparse, tree-structured explanation highlighting key decision factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable framework for generating model-agnostic explanations for LLMs and other models on text and structured data. It will provide both algorithmic advances in efficient explanation search and empirical validation, via human studies, that structured explanations improve task performance and trust over baseline methods.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the search for sufficient explanation sets with a human-feedback-driven optimization loop, moving beyond static feature importance scores to adaptive, user-aware explanatory summaries. This directly addresses the 'atypicality' problem by constructing explanations that account for feature interactions inherent in complex data.

proposals.title: Calibrating Trust: A Human-Subject Study on the Utility of Atypicality-Based Explanations for LLM Outputs
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes decision-support systems, user trust and appropriate reliance are critical. Prior work, such as 'Beyond Confidence: Reliable Models Should Also Consider Atypicality', shows that model confidence alone is a poor indicator of reliability. However, the practical utility of providing atypicality or other model-internal explanations to end-users for improving trust calibration and decision-making remains an open empirical question.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing users with explanations based on model atypicality (complementing confidence scores) will lead to better-calibrated trust and more accurate human+LLM collaborative decisions compared to providing confidence scores alone or no explanation.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled human-subject experiment using a platform like AlpacaFarm for simulation and data collection. Participants will perform text-based judgment tasks (e.g., claim verification, advice assessment) with support from an LLM. We will create three explanation conditions: 1) confidence score only, 2) confidence plus an atypicality score (calculated per 'Beyond Confidence'), and 3) a control with no score. We will measure decision accuracy, self-reported trust, and reliance patterns (acceptance/rejection of LLM suggestions) across explanation types and task difficulty levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide rigorous, empirical evidence on how specific, post-hoc explanatory features (atypicality) influence human-AI collaboration. It will yield concrete design guidelines for explanation interfaces that aim to calibrate user trust. The findings will directly inform the training and deployment of reliable, human-in-the-loop systems that learn from feedback.
proposals.novelty: While studies on explainable AI (XAI) exist, few pre-2026 works rigorously test the utility of *atypicality* as an explanatory signal for end-users. This bridges the gap between a novel reliability metric (atypicality) and human-computer interaction research on trust calibration.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization for Human-Aligned Explanations (BOHAE): Efficiently Tuning LLM Explanations via Preference Feedback
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes settings, generating explanations that are both faithful to the model's reasoning and useful to human users is critical. Current methods, like instruction-following fine-tuning or post-hoc generation, often produce verbose, inconsistent, or unhelpful explanations without a systematic way to optimize for human utility. This creates a gap between model performance and user trust, especially in domains like healthcare or finance where interpretability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the search for optimal, human-preferable explanations from an LLM can be formulated as a sample-efficient optimization problem over a latent or prompt space, and that Bayesian Optimization (BO) with a human-in-the-loop feedback signal can effectively solve it, outperforming standard instruction-tuning in both explanation quality and data efficiency.
proposals.method: We will frame explanation generation as optimizing a black-box utility function defined by human preferences. For a given model input, we treat the explanation-generating process (e.g., a prompt to an instruction-tuned LLM or parameters of a separate explainer module) as the optimization variables. Using a simulation framework like AlpacaFarm to model human feedback, we will employ a BO loop with a Gaussian Process surrogate model. In each batch, candidate explanations are sampled, their utility is estimated via simulated human preference rankings, and the surrogate is updated to guide the search toward higher-utility regions, balancing exploration and exploitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled optimization framework for aligning LLM explanations with human preferences, moving beyond simple supervised fine-tuning. It will provide concrete algorithms and an open-source benchmark for sample-efficient explanation tuning, advancing the field of human-AI interaction and reliable model communication.
proposals.novelty: While BO is used for hyperparameter tuning and human-in-the-loop ML, its application to directly optimize the semantic content and style of LLM explanations for human utility is novel. This bridges Guestrin's interests in reliable models (considering atypicality) and learning from human feedback, but applies it specifically to the explanation modality itself.

proposals.title: StreamSHAP: Efficient, Model-Agnostic Explanations for High-Velocity Data Streams
proposals.motivation: As machine learning is increasingly deployed in real-time decision systems (e.g., fraud detection, IoT monitoring), the need for explainability on streaming data is critical for trust and debugging. However, state-of-the-art model-agnostic explanation methods like SHAP and LIME are computationally intensive and designed for static datasets, making them infeasible for high-velocity streams where both data and concept drift occur.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting incremental learning techniques and exploiting the temporal locality of streaming data, we can develop a model-agnostic explanation framework that provides faithful, real-time feature attributions with sub-linear update complexity relative to stream length.
proposals.method: We will adapt the KernelSHAP algorithm for streaming contexts. Our core technical approach involves: 1) Maintaining a dynamic, reservoir-sampled background dataset that evolves with the stream to approximate the data distribution efficiently. 2) Implementing an incremental approximation for Shapley values by updating explanations only when new data significantly alters the conditional expectations, using a drift detection mechanism. 3) Leveraging the 'sets' and 'batch' processing themes from Guestrin's prior work to design mini-batch update rules that balance explanation fidelity with latency constraints, potentially using influence-function-based approximations for LLMs or other complex models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a first-of-its-kind, open-source framework for real-time, model-agnostic explanations on data streams. It will provide both a theoretical analysis of the trade-offs between efficiency and explanation quality in streaming settings and empirical validation on benchmarks from domains like network security and financial transactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on explaining static models or building inherently interpretable streaming models, the non-obvious challenge we address is decoupling explanation generation from model training, enabling real-time explanations for any black-box model on a stream, which is largely unexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inverse Explanation Alignment: Training Models to Adhere to Specified Local Explanations
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains, ensuring their decision-making aligns with human-understandable rationales is critical. Current methods like instruction tuning and RLHF optimize for final outputs but provide limited, post-hoc control over the model's internal reasoning pathways. This work addresses the inverse problem: directly shaping a model's local behavior to match a set of desired feature attributions or explanations, enabling more trustworthy and auditable AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A model can be effectively regularized or synthesized to produce predictions whose local explanations (e.g., SHAP, LIME, or gradient-based attributions) closely match a pre-specified set of explanation targets, thereby creating models with human-steerable reasoning patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training framework that integrates explanation fidelity as a core objective. Building on the AlpacaFarm simulation environment, we will create a pipeline where, for a batch of inputs, we compute the divergence between the model's current local explanations and the target explanations. This divergence, measured via metrics like weighted L2 norm on attribution vectors, will be used as a regularization loss during fine-tuning or as a reward signal in a reinforcement learning loop, inspired by RLHF but applied to explanation space rather than final output preference.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a new paradigm for model alignment that operates on the level of explanations, not just outputs. It will yield a practical method for injecting domain knowledge or safety constraints into models via explicable rules and contribute to the field of reliable AI by making model reasoning more directly programmable and verifiable.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses explanations to analyze models, we invert this pipeline to use explanations as a direct training signal. This moves beyond post-hoc interpretability to proactive explanation-guided model synthesis, a non-obvious extension of alignment techniques like RLHF.

proposals.title: From Local Explanations to Global Rules: Aggregating Post-Hoc Rationales for Interpretable Model Distillation
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) and complex predictors become ubiquitous, post-hoc explanation methods like LIME or SHAP provide crucial local insights but fail to yield a consistent, global understanding of model behavior. This fragmentation hinders trust, debugging, and the extraction of reliable, human-comprehensible knowledge from trained models. A method to compile these many local rationales into a compact rule set would bridge the gap between local explainability and global interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that local explanation sets generated for a model's predictions on a diverse data batch can be algorithmically aggregated and refined into a small set of simple, globally consistent logical rules that faithfully approximate the model's decision boundaries while being human-interpretable.
proposals.method: We will leverage a two-stage framework: (1) Generation: For a diverse batch of inputs, we will use instruction-tuned LLMs (e.g., based on AlpacaFarm principles) to produce natural language rationales for a target model's predictions, creating a dataset of (input, output, rationale) tuples. (2) Aggregation & Distillation: We will treat the rationale set as a corpus to be summarized, employing conceptual clustering and symbolic synthesis techniques. Inspired by 'Beyond Confidence: Reliable Models Should Also Consider Atypicality', we will prioritize rules that cover high-density, typical regions of the data while flagging atypical cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel pipeline for distilling black-box model behavior into auditable rule sets, advancing beyond one-off explanations. It will provide a concrete method to assess and improve the global consistency of explanations, a key concern for reliable AI. The output will be a form of interpretable model that can be validated and edited by humans.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates local explanations or trains inherently interpretable models, our approach is novel in directly aggregating post-hoc rationales into a global rule set, using LLMs as both explanation generators and refinement tools within a constrained synthesis loop. This moves explanation from a diagnostic tool to a constructive distillation mechanism.

proposals.title: Explaining Model Divergence and Consensus: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Model Comparison and Ensemble Rationalization
proposals.motivation: As LLMs proliferate, practitioners must choose between models or deploy ensembles, but lack tools to understand *why* models disagree or agree on specific predictions. This opacity hinders debugging, trust, and effective human-AI collaboration. Existing interpretability work focuses on single-model explanations, leaving a critical gap in comparative and collective reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging contrastive explanations and simulating human feedback on model disagreements, we can generate faithful, actionable rationales for model divergence and ensemble predictions that improve human decision-making over blind aggregation.
proposals.method: We will extend the AlpacaFarm simulation framework to generate synthetic preference data for contrasting model outputs on the same inputs. Using instruction-tuned LLMs, we will train an 'explainer' model to produce contrastive rationales, conditioned on input, outputs from multiple models (e.g., from a diverse set), and their performance metrics. For ensembles, we will adapt methods from 'Beyond Confidence: Reliable Models Should Also Consider Atypicality' to attribute predictions to member models and generate consensus explanations.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework and benchmark for generating contrastive and ensemble explanations. 2) Empirical evidence, via human studies, that such explanations improve model selection and error identification over using raw outputs or confidence scores. 3) Release of a simulation environment for training and evaluating comparative explainers.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond explaining a single model's internals to explaining the *relationships* between models, a crucial step for real-world deployment where multiple models are available. It uniquely combines contrastive explanation generation with human-feedback simulation specifically for model comparison.

ideas.title: Scale LIME to massive neural networks
ideas.description: Develop efficient, approximate versions of LIME for explaining predictions from very large-scale deep learning models.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate robust models
ideas.description: Use the test suite as adversarial training data to build models that pass the tests.
ideas.sourceTitle: Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList

ideas.title: Explanations for structured and text data
ideas.description: Extend model-agnostic interpretability methods to complex data types like text and structured inputs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Explanations need not be sparse
ideas.description: Investigate the utility of dense, non-sparse explanations for certain tasks or users.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Causal Intervention Design for Counterfactual Explanations
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology of causal intervention simulation from epidemiology to the problem of generating valid perturbations for model explanation. In epidemiology, structural causal models are used to simulate the effects of hypothetical interventions (like a new drug or policy) to estimate counterfactual outcomes in a population. The analogous solution involves formally specifying a causal graph, applying 'do-calculus' to set an intervention variable, and propagating the change through the model's structural equations. Adapted to machine learning, we propose learning or assuming a causal model that describes the data-generating process of the input features. To explain a model's prediction for a given instance, we generate perturbations not by random noise or gradient-based steps, but by performing a causal 'do-intervention' on a feature within this SCM. The resulting counterfactual instance, created by propagating the intervention, is a perturbation that inherently respects the causal structure of the data. This ensures that the contrast between the original and perturbed input—and the resulting change in the model's prediction—forms a causally grounded explanation, moving beyond correlational feature importance to answer 'what-if' questions that are valid within the true generative process.

ideas.title: Structure-Constrained Anchors via Adversarial Counterfactual Sampling
ideas.description: This system generates robust, interpretable anchors for structured data by solving the invalid perturbation problem. It integrates a generative adversary, trained on the same domain, to produce plausible counterfactual samples that respect the data's structural and semantic constraints. The anchor algorithm then operates over this constrained space of valid perturbations, ensuring that the identified decision rules are both precise and meaningful. This results in faithful explanations applicable to complex models for text, time series, and graph data.

ideas.title: Adaptive Query-by-Committee for Efficient Anchor Validation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Query-by-Committee (QBC) active learning strategy, commonly used in computational chemistry for efficient drug candidate screening, to the problem of anchor validation in explainable AI. In the analogous field, a committee of diverse predictive models selects molecular compounds for costly physical testing based on maximal disagreement among the committee's predictions, thereby optimizing the information gained per experiment. Adapted to anchor search, we maintain a committee of diverse interpretable models (e.g., decision trees, rule sets, linear surrogates) that all provide plausible explanations for the black-box model's local decision. When evaluating candidate anchor perturbations, the system calculates the disagreement among the committee members on whether the anchor condition remains satisfied. The perturbations eliciting the highest committee disagreement are selected for priority validation via direct queries to the black-box model. This approach strategically uses limited query budgets to resolve the greatest uncertainty first, leading to more efficient and reliable convergence on a precise anchor explanation.

ideas.title: Apply SGHMC to more large-scale Bayesian models
ideas.description: Demonstrate SGHMC on other big data problems beyond neural networks and matrix factorization.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

ideas.title: Explain model comparisons and ensembles
ideas.description: Generate explanations for why one model differs from another, or for ensemble predictions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Resource-Constrained Program Synthesis for Explainable Anchors
ideas.description: This research direction combines lightweight anchor generation with program synthesis to create minimal, executable programs that serve as local explanations for a model's prediction. By framing the anchor discovery as a program synthesis problem with explicit constraints on computational complexity (e.g., memory, operations, code size), the resulting explanations are not only interpretable but are also directly deployable on low-resource edge devices. This enables the explanation itself to run efficiently on the same constrained hardware as the model, supporting real-time, on-device interpretability. The core synergy is that the synthesis process is guided by the target platform's resource limits, producing explanations that are both functionally accurate and architecturally optimal for the edge.

ideas.title: Real-time explanations for streaming data
ideas.description: Adapt model-agnostic methods to provide explanations for predictions on high-velocity data streams.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Adaptively Learned Uncertainty Sets for Performance-Guaranteed Detection
ideas.description: This approach embeds robust optimization within a larger adaptive learning loop to overcome the critical limitation of static, pre-defined uncertainty sets. The system uses a two-tiered strategy: an inner loop performs robust optimization against a current uncertainty set to guarantee worst-case performance, while an outer loop employs adversarial exploration and online monitoring to identify performance gaps and novel uncertainties. These discovered failure modes are then used to rigorously expand or reshape the uncertainty set, ensuring the robustness guarantees evolve to cover a growing threat model. The result is a detection system that maintains mathematical performance guarantees while becoming progressively more robust to real-world complexities.

ideas.title: Anchors for model ensembles or cascades
ideas.description: Explain predictions from complex systems combining multiple models, not just single black-box models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Robust Anchors with Probabilistic Guarantees for Continuous Features
ideas.description: This method generates explanations for model predictions on continuous data by identifying high-density, contiguous regions in the feature space where the prediction is stable with high probability. Instead of brittle single-point rules, it constructs 'anchoring regions' defined by soft constraints and provides a statistical guarantee (e.g., 95% precision) that the model's prediction holds within the region. This ensures explanations are robust to small input perturbations and provides users with a reliable, understandable characterization of the model's behavior in the original continuous domain.

ideas.title: Sample-Efficient Anchors: Theory-Guided Design for Resource-Constrained Explainability
ideas.description: This research direction unifies theoretical analysis and practical deployment by developing anchor algorithms whose lightweight architecture is co-designed with their sample efficiency guarantees. The core innovation is to derive new, tighter bounds on anchor sample complexity that are not just descriptive but prescriptive, explicitly guiding choices like candidate rule pruning, adaptive sampling schedules, and approximation tolerance to minimize resource consumption. This results in a new class of anchor algorithms that are both theoretically grounded for high-precision guarantees and practically viable for low-resource environments, as the theory dictates the minimal necessary computation. The outcome is a principled methodology for deploying trustworthy, on-device explainable AI where the explanation's reliability is mathematically assured within the device's strict resource budget.

ideas.title: Dual: test the training data
ideas.description: Apply behavioral test concepts to audit and identify shortcomings in training datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList

ideas.title: Automatic model correction using explanations
ideas.description: Use LIME explanations not just for insight but to automatically correct or improve faulty models.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Scale down for small-data, high-precision inference
ideas.description: Adapt SGHMC for problems where gradient noise is small but computational budget is limited.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

ideas.title: Anchors via Internal State Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that anchor explanations require the ability to query a model with many perturbed inputs. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics—where scientists infer the internal structure of stars and black holes from passive observation of emitted signals rather than physical experimentation—we propose a method to compute anchor explanations without perturbation access. Instead of sending perturbed inputs to the model, we intensively analyze the internal state (activations, gradients, attention) of a single forward pass for the original input. From this, we build a lightweight, local surrogate model that maps hypothesized input perturbations to predicted output changes, simulating the perturbation process computationally. This allows for the identification of anchor features (sufficient conditions for the prediction) purely through internal state reconstruction and simulation, bypassing the need for explicit, restricted queries.

ideas.title: Extend to more complex graph structures
ideas.description: Apply the memory-efficient training algorithm to networks with more complex, non-linear computation graphs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Training Deep Nets with Sublinear Memory Cost

ideas.title: Relax the local sufficiency condition
ideas.description: Develop explanations that are probabilistically necessary, not just sufficient, for a prediction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Boosting without additive tree models
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that the base learners must be additive regression trees.

ideas.title: Extend LIME to structured data like tabular
ideas.description: Apply the LIME framework to generate explanations for predictions on structured, tabular data beyond text and images.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Minimal explanation for model debugging
ideas.description: Scale down the problem: find the minimal set of explanations needed to identify a specific model flaw or bias.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: One explanation does not fit all users
ideas.description: Generate personalized explanations tailored to different user expertise levels and goals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Topological Anchors: Constructing Interpretable Rules via Manifold Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that interpretable rules for machine learning models require discrete feature bins or thresholds. By drawing an analogy from computational topology, where continuous manifolds are represented by combinatorial structures like simplicial complexes, we propose a novel method for creating 'anchors' in non-discrete feature spaces. The analogous solution involves sampling the data manifold and building a mesh (e.g., an alpha shape or witness complex) that approximates its continuous shape with simple geometric pieces. Adapted to this context, we would sample the feature space to construct a simplicial complex, using its vertices as stable reference 'anchors.' An interpretable rule is then defined as a region in the original continuous space—such as the convex hull or a topological neighborhood of a subset of these anchors. This approach generates precise, human-understandable rules (e.g., 'the prediction applies to points within this specific polyhedron defined by anchors A, B, and C') without resorting to lossy discretization, preserving the fidelity of the original feature space.

ideas.title: Distill explanations into simple rules
ideas.description: Compile many local explanations into a small, globally consistent set of interpretable rules.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Optimize for distributed multi-GPU training
ideas.description: Combine the sublinear memory algorithm with data and model parallelism across multiple GPUs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Training Deep Nets with Sublinear Memory Cost

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Online Learning with Shielded Sensor Stream Integration
ideas.description: This system integrates optimal sensor placement with an online learning architecture hardened against data poisoning and deceptive concept drift. It employs a multi-armed bandit or ensemble approach where one arm specializes in detecting adversarial anomalies and distributional shifts in the incoming sensor streams in real-time. Only data that passes through this protective shield, which uses conformal prediction and temporal consistency checks, is used to update the primary detection model, while suspicious data is quarantined for analysis. This ensures continuous learning from real-time data while maintaining robustness against intelligent adversaries and non-stationary environmental noise.

ideas.title: Influence Maximization for Black-Box Anchor Discovery
ideas.description: This research inverts the anchor explanation problem by framing a model's decision logic for a given input as a constrained influence diffusion process over its latent feature associations. Using only the model's static predictions on the original data distribution, we apply influence maximization algorithms to identify the minimal set of input features that, if held constant, would maximize the 'influence' (i.e., probability) of the model's specific prediction. This yields anchor-like explanations—sufficient conditions for the decision—purely through analytical means, bypassing the need for generating or querying perturbed inputs, which is crucial for restricted or non-differentiable models.

ideas.title: Challenge the need for full graph consistency
ideas.description: Explore if many ML algorithms can work correctly with eventual or partial consistency.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributed GraphLab: A Framework for Machine Learning in the Cloud

ideas.title: Global anchors for model behavior
ideas.description: Scale anchors to find high-precision rules that explain model behavior across large regions or globally.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Adapting Causal Graph Pruning for Anchor Generation in Dependent Feature Spaces
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to adapt anchor generation for data with strongly correlated or causally dependent features, challenging the standard assumption of feature independence. Drawing an analogy from structural biology's protein folding problem—where predicting a 3D structure from a linear chain of interdependent amino acids is intractable—we adapt the solution of 'contact map-guided fragment assembly.' In that field, a sparse map of high-confidence residue-residue contacts is first inferred to constrain the vast conformational space. Analogously, for machine learning explanation, we propose to first learn a sparse causal or dependency graph among the model's features. This graph identifies coherent 'feature blocks' with strong internal dependencies but weaker external links. Anchor generation is then reformulated: instead of perturbing individual features independently, we intervene on these blocks as atomic units. This ensures that the synthetic data points used for anchor validation respect the real data's dependency structure, yielding explanations that are more faithful, stable, and representative of plausible data variations. The research would involve methods for robust dependency graph extraction from model/data and novel algorithms for block-wise anchor search and validation.

ideas.title: Incremental Anchors for Streaming, Non-Discrete Feature Spaces
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel incremental algorithm for the Anchor explainability framework that operates directly on raw, non-discrete feature spaces in a streaming data context. It eliminates the performance and fidelity loss from feature discretization, maintaining explanations as precise intervals or conditions in the original continuous space. The core innovation is a mechanism to efficiently update these precise anchors as new data arrives and the underlying model or data distribution evolves, ensuring explanations remain valid and stable over time. This creates a continuous, faithful, and adaptive explanation stream for black-box models deployed in non-stationary environments like financial tickers or sensor networks.

ideas.title: Use Bayesian optimization for explanations
ideas.description: Frame explanation search as an optimization problem solvable via Bayesian methods.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Apply to reinforcement learning training

ideas.description: Create scalable algorithms for the submodular pick step to handle very large datasets and model classes.

ideas.title: Extend theory to non-convex target distributions
ideas.description: Theoretical analysis of SGHMC performance for complex, multi-modal posterior distributions common in machine learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo

ideas.title: Relax the need for second-order gradient statistics
ideas.description: Develop a highly scalable boosting method that works efficiently with only first-order gradients.
ideas.sourceTitle: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Parsimony for Anchor Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from evolutionary biology's Maximum Parsimony method. In phylogenetics, given observed traits in species, parsimony finds the simplest evolutionary tree (requiring fewest changes) explaining the data. We adapt this to anchor discovery for model explanation. The problem is framed as synthesizing the minimal symbolic program (the 'anchor') that approximates the model's local decision. The 'observed data' is the model's input-output pairs in a local neighborhood. The synthesis algorithm, inspired by parsimony, searches the space of possible programs (combinations of feature conditions and operations) for the one that requires the fewest, simplest operations while maintaining high fidelity to the model's behavior, thereby producing a human-interpretable explanation of the local decision logic.

ideas.title: Globally Amortized Active Search for Efficient Anchor Generation and Validation
ideas.description: This approach combines active learning with a global, amortized model of the classifier's decision boundary to overcome the myopia of per-anchor optimization. A meta-acquisition function guides perturbation selection by balancing the local information gain for validating a specific anchor against the global utility of refining a shared surrogate model of the decision surface. This enables knowledge transfer across different anchor searches, progressively building a reusable approximation of the model's behavior in the feature space. Consequently, the system achieves far greater sample efficiency by reducing redundant queries and accelerating the validation of entire sets of anchors.

ideas.title: Robust Causal Perturbations via Agnostic Discovery and Multi-Model Ensembling
ideas.description: This system generates valid, process-respecting perturbations without requiring a pre-specified and perfect causal model. It first applies constraint-based or score-based causal discovery to learn a Markov equivalence class of plausible data-generating structures from the observational data. For explanation, it then computes perturbations that are consistent with all structures in this class, or it employs a conservative, assumption-lean approach using only direct parent-child relationships robustly identified. This results in explanations that are provably valid under a broad set of true underlying models, significantly increasing reliability in real-world settings where the full causal graph is unknown.

ideas.title: Global Phase Diagrams for Model Behavior via Renormalization Group Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts the renormalization group (RG) method from statistical physics to find global anchors for model behavior. In physics, RG coarse-grains a system to find scale-invariant fixed points and universal laws that govern behavior across phases. Analogously, we propose developing a computational RG procedure for neural networks. This involves iteratively abstracting or grouping a model's internal computations (e.g., across layers, attention heads, or feature clusters) to identify stable, scale-invariant functional motifs—'fixed points' of model behavior. These fixed points and their associated scaling relations (e.g., how error or sensitivity scales with model size or data complexity) would act as high-precision, global anchors. They would explain model behavior across large regions of input space, architectural variations, and task domains, creating a 'phase diagram' for AI model capabilities and vulnerabilities.

ideas.title: User studies on explanation utility
ideas.description: Conduct human-subject experiments to measure how explanations improve trust and decision-making.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Automate learning rate and parameter adaptation
ideas.description: Develop methods to automatically adjust the learning rate and other key parameters during the boosting process.
ideas.sourceTitle: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System

ideas.title: Leverage causal inference for perturbation
ideas.description: Generate perturbations using causal models to respect data-generating processes for valid explanations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Causally-Grounded Probabilistic Necessity Explanations
ideas.description: This approach develops explanations that are both probabilistically necessary and causally valid for model predictions. It combines statistical necessity measures with causal discovery techniques to identify features that are necessary in the data distribution and demonstrably causal through intervention analysis. The system explicitly tests for confounding relationships and uses counterfactual reasoning to eliminate spurious probabilistic necessities. This produces explanations that are robust to distribution shifts and reflect genuine causal mechanisms rather than statistical artifacts.

ideas.title: Scale up to model parameter memory
ideas.description: Extend the trade-off principle to also reduce memory for storing model parameters.
ideas.sourceTitle: Training Deep Nets with Sublinear Memory Cost

ideas.title: Inverse problem: From explanation to model
ideas.description: Given a set of desired local explanations, synthesize or regularize a model to match them.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Extend anchors to structured and sequential data
ideas.description: Apply the anchor explanation framework to models for text, time series, or graph-structured data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Incremental Game-Theoretic Anchors for Adaptive Detection in Non-Stationary Streams
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for online, adversarial model anchoring in non-stationary data streams. It formulates the continuous update of model anchors (interpretable decision rules) as a repeated game between a detector deploying sensors/algorithms and an evasive contaminant or adversary trying to avoid detection. Incremental algorithms will compute equilibrium strategies for anchor placement and adjustment, ensuring the model remains robust and interpretable despite the adversary's adaptive attempts to shift the data distribution. This enables real-time, strategic model adaptation that anticipates and counters intelligent evasion, going beyond passive drift adaptation.

ideas.title: Anchors for Structured Data via Causal Pathway Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from epidemiology and causal inference to extend the anchor explanation framework to structured and sequential data. In epidemiology, a 'sufficient cause component' is a minimal set of factors that reliably produces a disease outcome. Analogously, for a model operating on text, time series, or graph data, we define a 'structured anchor' as a minimal, connected substructure (like a key subgraph, a contiguous text span, or a temporal pattern) whose presence in an instance ensures the model's specific prediction with high probability. The adapted research idea involves developing new algorithms to efficiently search for these minimal sufficient substructures within the complex input space, and to statistically validate that they indeed act as probabilistic guarantees for the model's output, thereby providing interpretable, faithful explanations for black-box models on non-tabular data.

ideas.title: Interpretable features are user-independent
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that 'interpretable components' are universal; they may vary across different users or domains.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Adapt program synthesis for explanation
ideas.description: Frame anchor discovery as synthesizing a minimal program that approximates the model's local decision.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Real-Time, Interpretable Anomaly Detection in Sensor Networks via Sequential Anchors
ideas.description: This research fuses the anchor explanation framework with real-time sensor data streams to develop a novel online learning system for interpretable anomaly detection. It extends anchors to handle the sequential and structured nature of time-series sensor data and graph-based network dependencies. The system dynamically learns optimal 'explanation policies' that identify minimal, sufficient subsequences or sensor sub-graphs that cause an anomaly alert, updating these anchors in real-time as new data streams in. This enables operators to immediately understand the spatiotemporal precursors to failures or intrusions, moving beyond simple detection to actionable, explainable diagnostics for complex cyber-physical systems.

ideas.title: Interactive Anchors for Debugging and Trust in Personalized Outbreak Detection Systems
ideas.description: This research fuses personalized biosurveillance with human-centered model debugging to develop interactive systems for micro-community outbreak detection. It focuses on creating anchor-based explanations (e.g., 'The model suspects an outbreak because Sensor X and Y both reported anomalous readings') that allow public health officials or community leaders to understand, question, and correct the model's reasoning. Through user studies, we evaluate how these interactive anchors enable stakeholders to debug false alarms, suggest better sensor locations based on local knowledge, and ultimately build a trusted, co-designed detection system tailored to their unique environment and constraints.

ideas.title: Dual: Finding instances that break anchors
ideas.description: Identify or generate instances where a previously valid anchor fails, revealing model decision boundaries.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Testing is for evaluation, not training
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that behavioral tests are only for assessment, not for model improvement.
ideas.sourceTitle: Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList

ideas.title: Robust Symbolic Optimization for Certifiable Rule-Based Systems
ideas.description: This research direction integrates robust optimization and symbolic reasoning to develop a framework for designing certifiable, rule-based detection systems. The core method uses constraint programming to search the space of minimal rule structures while embedding robust optimization constraints that ensure each candidate rule set maintains its performance under worst-case perturbations of its input features or parameters. This synergy produces compact, interpretable models with explicit, quantifiable guarantees on their robustness, bridging a key gap between the efficiency of symbolic search and the reliability assurances of robust optimization. The resulting systems are particularly valuable for safety-critical applications where both model simplicity and guaranteed performance under uncertainty are required.

ideas.title: Single-machine optimization for terabyte-scale data
ideas.description: Push the limits of a single machine's memory and disk to train on datasets too big for RAM.
ideas.sourceTitle: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System

ideas.title: Inverse: maximize memory for speed

ideas.title: Real-time incremental tree boosting

ideas.sourceTitle: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System

ideas.title: Global Game-Theoretic Anchors for Strategic Sensor Network Design
ideas.description: This research synthesizes game-theoretic models for adversarial sensor placement with the concept of scalable global anchors. The core idea is to first use interpretability or simulation methods to identify a sparse set of high-precision, globally valid rules (anchors) that describe the fundamental behavior of a contaminant dispersion or evasion process. These anchors then formally define the strategy spaces and utility functions for a two-player game between a network planner and an evasive agent. By solving this anchored game, the method yields optimal, robust sensor configurations that are explainable through the underlying global behavioral principles, moving beyond heuristic placement or locally optimal solutions.

ideas.title: Bugs are independent and additive
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that test failures represent independent, non-interacting model flaws.
ideas.sourceTitle: Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList

ideas.title: Semantically-Anchored Rule Refinement via Symbolic-Statistical Co-Refinement
ideas.description: This system combines SAT solvers for efficient combinatorial search over rule spaces with a statistical validation framework to ensure semantic quality. The symbolic engine generates candidate minimal rule sets satisfying logical constraints, which are then evaluated for stability on validation data and predictive consistency. The statistical feedback, including measures like confidence intervals and permutation importance, is used to formulate new constraints, guiding subsequent symbolic search iterations. This co-refinement process converges on rules that are not only formally minimal but also robust, interpretable, and generalizable.

ideas.title: Use incremental checkpointing from HPC
ideas.description: Adapt incremental checkpointing techniques from high-performance computing for neural networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Training Deep Nets with Sublinear Memory Cost

ideas.title: Anchored Debugging Protocols: Adapting Surgical Safety Checklists for Model Debugging Workflows
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of user studies on anchor-based model debugging by drawing an analogy from surgical medicine's solution to complex, high-stakes debugging: the patient safety checklist. The analogous field is Surgical Medicine and Patient Safety, where the solution was the implementation of standardized, phase-based surgical safety checklists (like the WHO's) that act as cognitive and procedural anchors to prevent errors and standardize communication. Adapting this solution, the research would design and empirically evaluate a structured 'Anchored Debugging Protocol'—a checklist of key anchor-based verification steps—integrated into practical user workflows for debugging complex models. The study would investigate if this protocolized approach helps users more systematically and reliably debug models, reduce oversight errors, and build trust, analogous to how surgical checklists improved outcomes and team reliability in clinical settings.

ideas.title: Causal Inference from Probabilistic Necessity via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling (ENM) to challenge the assumption that explanations must be locally sufficient. In ecology, predicting exact species presence with sufficient local conditions is flawed due to historical and biotic factors. Instead, ENM infers the necessary environmental constraints (the fundamental niche) from occurrence data. Similarly, for a given model prediction, we propose to learn a 'fundamental explanatory niche'—a probabilistic description of the input feature constraints that are necessary for that prediction. The method would invert the explanatory goal: rather than identifying features that alone can cause the prediction (sufficiency), it would statistically identify feature value ranges whose absence makes the prediction probabilistically impossible or highly unlikely. This yields explanations framed as necessary conditions ('prediction Y rarely occurs without X being in range R'), which could be more robust and generalizable than local, sufficient-attribution methods.

ideas.title: User studies on anchor-based model debugging
ideas.description: Evaluate how anchors help users debug, improve, or build trust in complex models in practical workflows.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Support a wider range of consistency models
ideas.description: Offer tunable consistency levels to trade off performance for algorithmic correctness needs.

ideas.title: Invert the black-box assumption
ideas.description: Use interpretability to guide the design of inherently transparent models, not just explain black boxes.

ideas.title: Incorporate cognitive bias checklists
ideas.description: Adapt checklists from cognitive psychology to test for heuristic biases in model predictions.

ideas.title: Apply active learning to anchor search

ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Causal Field Evaluation of Anchors for Sustainable Debugging and Trust Calibration
ideas.description: This research moves beyond short-term lab studies to conduct a longitudinal field experiment that measures the causal impact of anchor-based explanations on real-world model debugging. Developers are integrated into a controlled but authentic workflow environment and randomly assigned to use either a genuine anchor interface or a blinded placebo tool over an extended period. The study quantifies objective debugging performance on seeded model errors and captures behavioral trust metrics, isolating the tool's true effect from bias and novelty. The outcome is a robust, ecologically valid assessment of whether anchors lead to sustained improvements in debugging efficiency and appropriately calibrated trust.

ideas.title: Optimize memory for non-sequential activations
ideas.description: Focus on reducing memory for layers with large, non-sequential activation maps like 3D convolutions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Training Deep Nets with Sublinear Memory Cost

ideas.title: Adaptive Preference Learning for Outbreak Detection Under Value Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic sensor placement framework that explicitly accounts for uncertainty in public health priorities and detection objectives during an emerging outbreak. Instead of requiring fixed weights, the system interactively learns decision-maker preferences through scenario-based feedback and adjusts surveillance strategies in near-real time. It integrates models of outbreak spread, sensor performance, and value drift to propose robust network configurations that perform well across a range of potential future preference states. The outcome is an adaptive tool that maintains detection efficacy even as operational goals and societal trade-offs shift during a crisis.

ideas.title: Explainable Anomaly Detection in Streaming Networks with Dependent Features
ideas.description: This research develops a novel framework for real-time outbreak detection in massive, streaming graph data (e.g., financial transactions, social media) where storing the entire network is impossible. It advances beyond simple anomaly scoring by integrating explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically adapted to generate interpretable 'anchors' or rules that explain why a subgraph is flagged as anomalous. The core innovation lies in modifying these explanation methods to respect and leverage the strong temporal, correlational, and causal dependencies between network features and edges that emerge in the stream, ensuring explanations are both faithful to the complex data dynamics and computable under strict memory constraints.

ideas.title: Active Learning for Multi-Objective Anchor Search in Outbreak Detection
ideas.description: This research direction integrates active learning with multi-criteria optimization to create a unified framework for designing and validating outbreak detection systems. The core idea is to first formulate the sensor placement problem as a multi-objective optimization to generate a Pareto front of candidate 'anchor' detection strategies, each representing a different trade-off between objectives like detection speed, specificity, and resource cost. An active learning meta-algorithm then sequentially selects the most informative simulated or historical outbreak scenarios to test these candidate anchors, efficiently reducing uncertainty about their real-world performance across all objectives simultaneously. This closed-loop process enables the adaptive refinement of both the detection system design and its validation, ensuring the final deployed strategy is robust and Pareto-optimal under practical constraints.

ideas.title: Explain models with non-independent features
ideas.description: Adapt anchor generation for data where features have strong correlations or causal dependencies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Anchors: High-Precision Model-Agnostic Explanations

ideas.title: Test cases require minimal human effort
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that creating effective test templates is low-effort for all users.

ideas.title: Local linearity is not required
ideas.description: Create explanations using non-linear, more complex local surrogate models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Model-Agnostic Interpretability of Machine Learning

ideas.title: Apply robust statistics to explanation stability
ideas.description: Use robust statistical methods to make local explanations less sensitive to perturbation noise and sampling variance.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Use causal inference for faithful explanations
ideas.description: Incorporate causal inference techniques to distinguish correlation from causation in feature attribution explanations.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Local linearity may not hold globally
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that a locally linear explanation is sufficient for all complex, highly non-linear decision boundaries.
ideas.sourceTitle: “Why Should I Trust You?”: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier

ideas.title: Active Compressed Sensing for Efficient Causal Network Inference in Epidemics
ideas.description: This research synthesizes compressed sensing and active learning to develop an adaptive framework for inferring epidemic transmission networks from extremely sparse sensor data. The core innovation is using active learning strategies to sequentially select the most informative geographic locations or population groups for sparse monitoring, guided by the uncertainty and reconstruction errors of a compressed sensing model. This closed-loop, query-by-committee approach allows for the efficient recovery of the full outbreak pattern and, crucially, the active testing of hypothesized causal anchors (key transmission links) through targeted perturbation queries. The result is a highly data-efficient method that simultaneously reconstructs the epidemic state and validates the causal structure of its underlying transmission network.

ideas.title: Incremental Anchor-Based Sensor Placement for Evolving Data Streams
ideas.description: This research develops incremental algorithms that jointly update a set of 'anchor' nodes—interpretable exemplars of core data patterns—and an optimal sensor placement strategy on a network whose structure and data distribution are both non-stationary. The core innovation is to treat the anchor-finding and sensor-placement problems as a coupled, online optimization task, where new streaming data dictates updates to the latent model (anchors), which in turn informs cost-effective sensor repositioning to best capture the evolving system state. This enables adaptive monitoring infrastructures for applications like traffic networks or social media, where sensors are dynamically placed not just where the network is critical, but where the data is most representative of emerging trends or anomalies.
