ideas.title: Adaptive, Two-Tier Sketching for High-Fidelity Outbreak Detection in Streaming Graphs
ideas.description: This system introduces an adaptive verification mechanism to overcome the accuracy-scalability trade-off in streaming outbreak detection. It employs a primary, ultra-efficient sketch to screen the entire stream for potential outbreaks with high recall. Upon a soft alert, the system temporarily instantiates a dedicated, high-precision sketch focused precisely on the region of interest to confirm or reject the signal. This approach maintains constant, sublinear memory usage for routine monitoring while providing statistically rigorous, low-error verification for any candidate outbreak, making it suitable for high-stakes decision-making.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Anchor Representation Learning for Evolving Data Streams
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for maintaining a dynamic set of anchors that collectively represent the non-stationary data manifold in a streaming context. Instead of incrementally updating a single anchor, the system uses online clustering and concept drift detection to identify, create, and retire multiple anchors corresponding to distinct and recurring data concepts. The model's adaptation is then guided by this evolving anchor representation, enabling robust learning under complex drift patterns like gradual change, sudden shifts, or re-emerging contexts. This approach ensures the model's foundational references remain semantically meaningful and temporally relevant.

ideas.title: Robust, Data-Adaptive Sample Complexity for Anchor Reliability under Distribution Shift
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for determining the number of samples needed to achieve reliable anchor-based explanations with high probability in non-ideal, real-world settings. It explicitly models uncertainty in anchor identification and potential violations of the i.i.d. assumption, such as covariate shift or temporal drift. The core output is a set of adaptive, data-dependent bounds that practitioners can compute from their dataset to certify anchor reliability or determine if more data is required for a desired confidence level, thereby bridging theory and application.

ideas.title: Applying Statistical Physics Renormalization Methods to Anchor Sample Complexity
ideas.description: This research adapts renormalization group (RG) methods from statistical physics to provide tighter theoretical bounds on anchor sample complexity. In statistical physics, RG theory provides precise scaling laws for systems near critical points by analyzing how correlations behave under systematic coarse-graining. Analogously, we treat anchor learning as approaching a 'critical point' of perfect identification. The adapted approach constructs a renormalization procedure for the joint distribution of features and anchor functions, allowing us to track how identification precision scales with sample size under successive coarse-graining of the hypothesis space. This enables derivation of distribution-dependent scaling laws that provide substantially tighter bounds than worst-case analysis, particularly for distributions exhibiting hierarchical or multi-scale structure. The resulting framework would characterize how sample complexity depends on intrinsic properties of the data distribution and anchor function class, similar to how RG methods relate critical exponents to system dimensionality and symmetry.

ideas.title: Robust Source Identification Under Uncertainty via Adaptive Ensemble Influence Maximization
ideas.description: This system addresses the critical data uncertainty in early outbreaks by framing source identification as a robust optimization problem over an ensemble of plausible transmission networks and disease parameters. It continuously assimilates heterogeneous real-time data—such as case reports, mobility traces, and genomic sequences—to update probabilistic models of the contact network and transmission dynamics via a Bayesian inference engine. The core algorithm then executes influence maximization across this ensemble to identify candidate outbreak sources that are consistently influential under the most probable scenarios, providing a dynamic, uncertainty-aware ranking for targeted investigation and intervention.

ideas.title: Stressor-Aware Sensor Placement with Correlated Reliability Degradation
ideas.description: This approach models sensor networks where node reliability is not independent but dynamically co-evolves with shared environmental stressors, such as electromagnetic interference, weather fronts, or targeted adversarial conditions. The system optimizes sensor placement and network architecture to maintain coverage and detection confidence under spatially and temporally correlated failure modes. It employs robust optimization or stochastic programming techniques that account for worst-case stressor scenarios, ensuring the network design is resilient to systemic, not just random, reliability losses. This results in a deployment strategy that is fundamentally more robust for real-world, adversarial, or harsh environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Parsimony Methods for Rule Refinement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Maximum Parsimony principle from computational phylogenetics to the context of symbolic rule refinement. In phylogenetics, parsimony methods find the evolutionary tree requiring the fewest mutations to explain observed species data. Analogously, we frame rule refinement as finding the minimal set of modifications to a set of symbolic anchor rules needed to correctly classify all given training examples. By encoding the classification constraints of the examples and the possible rule modifications into a SAT or constraint satisfaction model, we can leverage efficient solvers to search for the most parsimonious (minimally satisfying) refined rule set, optimizing for rule simplicity and accuracy simultaneously.

ideas.title: Sparse Anchor Networks via Compressed Sensing Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from compressed sensing in signal processing to the challenge of creating lightweight anchor algorithms for resource-constrained environments. In compressed sensing, a sparse signal is accurately reconstructed from a small number of incoherent measurements, bypassing the need for high-rate sampling. Adapted here, we propose 'Sparse Anchor Networks': the algorithm on the edge device does not compute or store complete anchor sets. Instead, it takes a minimal set of randomized, linear projections from the input data. A very lightweight, sparsity-promoting optimization (e.g., a simplified iterative thresholding algorithm) then reconstructs only the few anchor points critical for the current decision. This drastically reduces computational load, memory footprint, and energy consumption, enabling anchor-based reasoning on microcontrollers and sensors by trading dense computation for sparse, on-demand reconstruction.

ideas.description: This research addresses the inverse sensor placement problem with a focus on real-world robustness. Instead of seeking a single minimal sensor set, it synthesizes adaptive sensor activation strategies and resilient placement configurations that guarantee a detection deadline even when sensor performance is probabilistic, the environment is dynamic, and targets are non-cooperative. The core advance is a framework that co-optimizes for minimal resource use and maximal tolerance to modeled uncertainties, providing performance guarantees under a wider set of operational conditions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Network Tuning via Cardiovascular Homeostasis Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the homeostatic control principles of the human cardiovascular baroreflex to the problem of integrating optimal sensor placement with real-time data streams. In physiology, baroreceptors provide continuous pressure readings to a central controller that makes autonomic adjustments to maintain system stability. Analogously, we propose a sensor network architecture where primary optimally-placed sensors stream detection confidence metrics to a central learning controller. This controller then executes real-time adjustments to secondary sensor sampling frequencies, activation thresholds, and data fusion parameters, creating a closed-loop system that continuously optimizes detection performance while minimizing resource consumption, mirroring how the body maintains blood pressure with minimal energy expenditure.

ideas.title: Robust and Stable Anchors for Low-Resource Deployment
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, lightweight anchor algorithm specifically engineered for the noisy and non-stationary data environments of edge devices. It incorporates stability regularization during the anchor search process and employs efficient, distribution-aware sampling to generate candidate anchors that are invariant to common input corruptions. The system provides a tunable robustness-efficiency trade-off, allowing deployment across a range of hardware constraints while certifying a bound on explanation variance, ensuring reliable interpretability in real-world low-resource settings.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sampling for Evolving Networks via Ecological Monitoring Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecological monitoring to the problem of dynamic sensor placement in evolving networks. In ecology, adaptive sampling strategies are used to update the placement of environmental monitoring stations over time in response to observed data and predicted environmental changes. Similarly, we propose developing algorithms that treat a changing network topology as a dynamic landscape. The algorithm would integrate a predictive model of network evolution (e.g., based on historical growth patterns or known external events) with real-time feedback from existing sensors. Using principles from sequential decision-making and model-based optimization, it would periodically output a reconfiguration plan—specifying where to add, remove, or relocate sensors—to maintain near-optimal performance for a specified monitoring task (like contamination detection or influence maximization) under constraints of budget and reconfiguration cost. This approach explicitly balances the exploration of newly important network regions with the exploitation of current sensor placements.

ideas.title: Robust and Adaptive Sensor Placement for Networks with Uncertain Evolution
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms for placing sensors in networks where the future topology and dynamics are uncertain. Instead of reactively updating placements after changes are detected, the approach proactively computes strategies that are robust to a wide range of possible evolutionary trajectories. The core innovation is a framework that optimizes for worst-case or risk-aware performance guarantees across modeled uncertainty sets, ensuring monitoring effectiveness even under unexpected changes. This results in more resilient and cost-effective long-term sensing infrastructures for critical systems like power grids or epidemic tracking networks.

ideas.title: Causal Sensor Placement for Guaranteed Detection via Valid Perturbation Testing
ideas.description: This research fuses causal inference with optimal sensor placement to solve the inverse problem of finding the smallest sensor set that guarantees detection of any significant system perturbation within a strict deadline. The key innovation is using a causal model of the system's data-generating process to create a constrained set of valid, realistic perturbations for testing candidate sensor networks, rather than relying on historical anomalies or arbitrary disturbances. This ensures the resulting minimal sensor configuration is robust to all plausible real-world events that respect the system's causal structure, providing a rigorous, efficiency-focused framework for safety-critical monitoring in complex physical, biological, or infrastructure networks.

ideas.title: Minimal Observability for Bounded-Time State Estimation
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'sensor placement for guaranteed observability' problem from control theory to the inverse problem of minimizing sensors for a guaranteed detection time. In the analogous field, solutions involve analyzing a system's dynamics (via tools like the observability Gramian) to place a minimal set of sensors ensuring state estimation within a time bound. The adapted idea treats the monitored domain as a dynamical system where critical events propagate according to known models (e.g., diffusion, wave, or network models). The core problem is to find the smallest set of sensor positions that guarantees any event will be detected within a predefined deadline T, formally ensuring the system is T-observable. This combines spatial optimization with temporal performance constraints from systems theory.

ideas.title: Symbolic Constraint Solving for Inverse Problems in Variable-Speed Spreading
ideas.description: This research direction combines variable-speed spreading processes with symbolic reasoning to solve the inverse problem of inferring the latent delay rules from observed propagation timelines. Instead of assuming fixed delays, we model the propagation delay between nodes as a function of their attributes and state, defined by a set of logical or arithmetic rules. A SAT solver or constraint programming engine is then used to search the space of possible rules, identifying the minimal set that is consistent with the observed event timestamps, thereby automatically reverse-engineering the dynamics of complex, adaptive contagion or diffusion systems.

ideas.title: Byzantine-Resilient Distributed Sensor Placement with Verifiable Local Consensus
ideas.description: This research develops decentralized protocols for sensor placement that are robust to adversarial behavior and strategic manipulation. The system requires nodes to cryptographically commit to their local utility assessments and proposed placements, which are then validated by a randomly sampled consensus group using lightweight proof-of-utility checks. This ensures the emergent global configuration is near-optimal while guaranteeing resilience against Sybil attacks, collusion, and Byzantine failures up to a proven threshold. The final protocol provides strong probabilistic guarantees on coverage and resource efficiency even in untrusted, open-network environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography's Sparse Array Inversion for Outbreak Pattern Recovery
ideas.description: This idea applies the core solution from seismic tomography—reconstructing a complex field from sparse sensor data via physics-informed, sparsity-regularized inversion—to the problem of monitoring disease outbreaks. In geophysics, the subsurface is imaged from a few seismometers by solving an inverse problem that incorporates wave physics and assumes a sparse representation of geological structures. Analogously, we propose to model outbreak propagation dynamics (e.g., via metapopulation or network models) as the 'forward physics.' By assuming the true outbreak pattern is sparse in an appropriate domain (such as a wavelet basis over space or a spectral basis over a contact network), compressed sensing principles can be leveraged. The adapted research idea is to develop an inversion framework that recovers the high-resolution, spatio-temporal origin and spread of an outbreak from a minimal set of sensor measurements (e.g., from sparse wastewater monitoring nodes or sentinel clinics), effectively 'tomographing' the outbreak from limited data.

ideas.title: Apply Robust Control's H-infinity Synthesis to Guarantee Detection Performance
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from robust control theory, specifically the H-infinity synthesis method used to guarantee controller performance under worst-case disturbances and model uncertainties. The analogous solution formulates control design as a minimax optimization problem to bound the worst-case system gain. Adapted to the research context of detection under model uncertainty, this involves structuring the detector design as an H-infinity optimization problem. The goal is to synthesize detector parameters or a pre-processing filter that explicitly minimizes the worst-case impact of bounded uncertainties (in the signal model, noise, or environment) on a defined detection performance criterion, thereby providing a rigorous, worst-case performance guarantee analogous to stability margins in control systems.

The analogous problem is tracking the state of a changing ecosystem (ecological succession) without exhaustive surveys, solved by monitoring key indicator species. The adapted research idea applies this to streaming data by defining 'indicator anchors' that signal the current data regime. An incremental algorithm will be developed to monitor these indicators for drift, updating the full anchor set efficiently only upon detecting a significant regime change, optimizing resource use for non-stationary data streams.

ideas.title: Variable-Speed Spreading with Adaptive Transmission Delays
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of fixed propagation delays in spreading models by drawing an analogy from traffic flow theory. In transportation, vehicle speeds and propagation delays between locations aren't fixed but depend dynamically on traffic density, road conditions, and driver behavior—modeled through fundamental diagrams and car-following models. Adapting this solution, we propose modeling information, influence, or contagion spreading with variable transmission delays that are functions of local network conditions: 'congestion' measured by active neighbor density, individual node processing capacities, and temporal receptiveness states. This creates spreading processes where wavefront speeds adapt dynamically to network load and structure, enabling more realistic modeling of phenomena like information saturation in social networks, variable-speed epidemic spread due to behavioral feedback, or computational task propagation in distributed systems with heterogeneous resources.

ideas.title: Distributed Sensor Placement via Reaction-Diffusion Pattern Formation
ideas.description: This research adapts the principles of biological morphogenesis and reaction-diffusion systems to solve the decentralized sensor placement problem. Inspired by how cells self-organize into spatial patterns (like animal coat markings) using only local chemical signaling, we propose a protocol where network nodes exchange virtual activator and inhibitor messages. Each node uses a local, stochastic rule to decide to activate as a sensor based on the relative strength of these signals, with the inhibitor diffusing faster or farther than the activator. This creates a local competition mechanism, resulting in emergent, near-optimal sensor placement with uniform coverage and minimal interference, achieved through decentralized collaboration without global knowledge or central coordination.
